Pocket 7s is an overpair on a 8h-2d-Td flop, strong top of our range after 3-betting preflop heads-up. Minni's calling range includes overcards (KJ, AQ, etc.), mid pairs, suited connectors/draws, but we're ahead of most. Board has diamond flush draw and gutshots (e.g., J9, 97), so betting denies equity and builds pot for value from worse hands like 99-TT, draws. OOP c-bet sizing ~2/3 pot (1200 into 1900) balances value/protection/fold equity against her button calling range. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/draws or facing a raise. History shows Minni calls multi-street but folds to aggression when behind.

I have top pair (Ten) with a weak kicker (T5s) and I’m in position against the preflop 3-bettor. Elon’s small c-bet (1,200 into a 3,100 pot) is a fairly wide/probing sizing — his range includes many missed overcards (AK/AQ), some medium pairs, and value 3-bet hands (TT/QQ/KK/AA). Calling is the best line: I keep his bluffs and worse hands in, realize my equity, and control the pot size with a vulnerable kicker. Raising here risks getting isolated by better pairs or a shove and is unnecessary to deny equity on a relatively dry board. Plan: call now, re-evaluate on the turn — value/call small turns or fold to heavy turn aggression from obvious overpairs/sets; bet for thin value if he checks and a safe river completes.

Pocket 7s remains a strong overpair on this turn board (8h 2d Td 2c), now pairing the deuces, which is a low card unlikely to have hit Minni's flop-calling range heavily (her range: Ax suited/broadway, 66-JJ, suited connectors/draws like J9s, QJs, 97s). The pairing doesn't complete many draws (diamond draw still live but turn club doesn't hit common ones), and it disfavors floaters/overcards while improving few hands like 22 or T2. History shows Minni is sticky post-flop but folds to sustained aggression when behind (e.g., Hands 13, 44, 47). OOP, betting continues value from worse pairs/draws (99-TT, Ax, missed draws), protects against any live equity, and builds pot for our equity edge. Checking risks giving a free card to draws or facing a bluff-raise we have to call. Sizing ~70% pot (3000 into 4300) extracts value from her calling station tendencies while leaving room for river flexibility and fold equity against pure air. Shoving too polarized/polarizes us thin; smaller bet optimal for max EV.

I have top pair (T♠5♠) with a weak kicker on 8h 2d Td 2c. Elon’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop c‑bet, turn bet/raise to 3000) is polarized toward very strong value (sets/boats/quads, better tens/overpairs) but still contains enough missed overcards and worse tens that my hand has real showdown value. Pot is 7300 and calling 3000 makes the pot 10300, so I need ~29% equity to break even; in position with a pair and some backdoor play I clear that threshold against a realistic turn‑raising range. Raising here would commit too much with a vulnerable kicker and folding surrenders too much equity. I’ll call 3000, keep worse hands and bluffs in, and re‑evaluate on the river (fold to a polarizing shove representing a full house/quads; call smaller river bets or any line where I improve).

Pocket 7s on this final board (8h 2d Td 2c Ks) remains a strong overpair to the 8/T/2s, beating Minni's likely calling range including Ax suited (missed diamond flush), weaker pairs (99, 66, Jx, Qx), floated broadways (KQ, AQ that missed), and bluffs/draws from her button defend vs 3bet. The paired 2s help by improving few hands in her range (sets unlikely), and river K improves some Kx but we still beat unpaired K-high or worse K-pairs often; our history of 3bet pre + double barrel OOP represents 88+/TT/sets/Ax strongly, so thin value from worse showdown hands and bluffs she stuck with (Minni's calling station tendencies evident in hands like 12,15,19,61,63,70,75 where she calls multi-street). Pot 10300 > our 2675 stack, so jamming maximizes pressure: extracts from worse (e.g., T8, 87s, KQo), gets folds from air/missed draws, denies her cheap showdown OOP. Checking risks her check-behind with worse or value-bet we snap-call; pure air unlikely after calling turn 3000. Effective stacks play: her 4695 covers. Optimal EV shove per villain history and board texture.

Elon 3‑bet preflop, barreled the flop and turned a very large bet and now peels a small river bet of 2,675 into a 12,975 pot. I currently have T♠5♠ — top pair with a weak kicker. Calling 2,675 would make the final pot 15,650 so my break‑even equity is ~2,675/15,650 ≈ 17.1%.

Range/read: his line is weighted to strong value (TT → full house on the turn, 22/quads, sets like 88, and overpairs AA/KK/QQ/JJ) but also contains some 3‑bet bluffs and broadway/AX combos that missed the turn and only pair a King on the river. His river sizing is relatively small vs the pot (and he’s effectively all‑in), so he’s trying to get called by weaker tens and some single‑pair hands rather than polarizing to only quads/full houses. That leaves a non‑trivial fraction of his river range that my hand beats (missed AK/AQ types, weaker pairs, and occasional air).

Decision: the call is cheap relative to the pot and my hand has real showdown value vs a realistic river range. Folding here surrenders too much equity. I cannot raise (he’s all‑in). I’ll call the 2,675 to see showdown. If I lose I’m left with ~2,020 behind, but this is the correct +EV decision given the pot odds and his line.

Opening on the button in heads-up play with position. 3-9o is a reasonable hand to open with from this spot given the wide button range in heads-up. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) is standard sizing. Looking to either win the blinds immediately or play heads-up post-flop with position advantage. Stack sizes are healthy and we have chip advantage.

Facing Claude’s button open to 250 (I’m BB), pot is 350 and I must call 150 (min-raise to 400). I have T♠2♥ — a weak, poorly-connected offsuit holding with very little postflop playability and few blockers. Although the raw pot odds to call are ~30%, playing T2o out of position on deep stacks versus a button opener will produce poor realized equity and create difficult OOP decisions. A tiny 3-bet here (to 400) won’t credibly fold enough of his range and a larger 3-bet with this hand is unnecessarily high-variance. Prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better spot, so fold the hand.

Heads-up on the button with K♣4♥. In a 2-player game position is huge and my opening range should be wide; K4o is marginal but perfectly acceptable to raise for fold equity and to play post‑flop in position. Stacks are deep (~175 BB) so I can maneuver if called, but I don’t want to bloat the pot unnecessarily with a marginal holding. I’ll raise to the legal min (200 total). If Claude folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I play post‑flop in position and c‑bet dry boards, and I’ll fold to a large 3‑bet since K4o is easily dominated.

In heads-up play with 6-8 offsuit in the big blind, I have adequate pot odds (3:1) to call Minni's raise. The hand has straight potential and is strong enough to see a flop given the expanded ranges in 2-player poker. With a chip advantage, I can apply position postflop as the big blind. This is a standard big blind defense play rather than a fold.

With 6-8 offsuit on a JJJ board, I have no draws and minimal hand strength. I'm first to act on the flop - checking is the standard play. This allows me to control pot size and see Minni's action before committing more chips. Any value hand from Minni will have the jacks; my hand is essentially a bluff catcher at best. Checking minimizes losses while keeping the option to fold if pressured.

Flop is Jd Js Jc — trips on board. Heads‑up, Claude’s preflop calling range contains a lot of pocket pairs and broadways; any pocket pair now becomes a full house and therefore dominates my Kc4h. My two cards provide almost no showdown value and only block a tiny number of full‑house combos (one K and one 4). Betting here would be a pure bluff into a range that is heavily weighted to full houses and will either call or check‑raise for very large value — not a profitable spot to bloat the pot with air, especially with deep stacks. I should pot‑control: check and fold to any realistic bet. If Claude checks back I lose a small pot without further investment; if he bets I’ll fold.

On the turn with 6c 8h on a board of JdJsJc9c, I have no made hand and no draws. This is a polarized board texture where most hands either have a jack or miss completely. Both players checked the flop, indicating weakness from both sides. The 9c on the turn doesn't improve my hand or change the dynamics significantly. Betting here would be an unmotivated bluff into a checking pattern on a board where my hand has almost no showdown value. Checking is the correct play to: (1) keep the pot small with a weak holding, (2) see the river card for free if Minni also checks, and (3) gather information about opponent's intentions. If Minni bets, I'll reassess with the pot odds and board context, but checking preserves value by allowing unprofitable bluffs to be avoided.

Flop came J♦ J♠ J♣ and the turn is 9♣. I hold K♣4♥ — no pair and essentially zero showdown equity versus Claude’s preflop calling range, which is heavily weighted to pocket pairs that are now full houses (and JJ would be quads). Betting the turn would be a pure bluff into a range that rarely folds and can easily check‑raise for massive value; with deep stacks that’s a terrible spot to bloat the pot with air. Claude checked the turn, so pot‑control is the right plan: check behind, preserve my stack, and fold to a bet on the river (unless it’s a very small block bet that offers absurd odds). If Claude checks back to the river I’ll reassess only if the river materially improves my hand (e.g. a king) or gives a clear cheap decision.

River action on a triple-jack board with 68o. I have no made hand, no draws, and zero showdown value against any reasonable holding. Both players have checked flop and turn, indicating weakness from both sides. Betting here would be an unmotivated bluff on a board texture where: (1) any jack beats me, (2) Minni likely folds nothing and calls with anything better, and (3) the risk-reward doesn't justify the bluff given my chip position. Checking allows me to see if Minni bets, and if she does, I can fold and minimize losses. If Minni also checks, we reach showdown where I can chop or lose the minimum with my weak holding. Checking is the disciplined play with this particular hand strength.

Preflop I opened K♣4♥ and Claude called. The board J♦ J♠ J♣ 9♣ 3♣ leaves almost all of Claude’s calling range (pocket pairs, broadways) turned into full houses or better; my K‑high has essentially no showdown value and I block almost nothing. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a very heavy full‑house range and is liable to be check‑raised for huge value. Claude checked the turn and checked the river, so the safest, most +EV line is pot‑control and take it to showdown rather than bloat the pot with air. I will check and preserve my stack (and fold to any river bet).

Heads-up play favors positional aggression. With Q6 on the button against a big blind, I have both positional and chip stack advantages. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) applies proper pressure and maintains my aggressive winning strategy. This hand has reasonable equity in heads-up play, and my position allows me to control the action. Minni will likely fold many hands, and when called, I'll have position postflop.

Heads-up vs Button open to 250 (pot 350). I have Q8s — a reasonably strong, suited hand in heads-up that plays well multi-street. Calling 150 (total commitment 250) is the best play: my break-even equity is 150/(350+150)=30%, and Q8s usually has at least that vs a wide button opening range. Stacks are deep (~177BB), so I have implied odds to realize equity and extract value when I connect. A min-raise to 400 is an option, but it doesn’t change my postflop position and just bloats the pot with a marginal hand; I’ll preserve stack flexibility and see a flop. Plan: continue when I hit (top pair, good draws), exercise pot control or fold to heavy aggression if I miss, and fold to a sizable 3‑bet preflop.

I flopped trips (8s) on 8h 6d 8d — a very strong hand heads-up against a wide button opener. The board has two diamonds, so there are meaningful diamond draws and some backdoor outs; I should both extract value and deny free cards. Betting sizes >~270 chips will make pure diamond draws indifferent or fold, so I’ll choose a sizable value/protection bet but not pot‑commit myself. Bet ~350 (≈70% pot) to charge Ax/Kx/Qx, medium pockets and draws; if he raises I’ll reassess (he could have the lone remaining 8 or a strong draw), if he calls I’ll continue extracting on safe runouts.

Calling on the flop with Q6h against Minni's raise. I have a pair of 6s with Q kicker and a backdoor flush draw on a paired 8-high board. While my hand is vulnerable to trips, better pairs, and better kickers, calling allows me to see another card and gather more information while maintaining control of the pot. The paired board somewhat reduces the likelihood of Minni having made a strong hand, and my position and stack advantage give me flexibility for future streets.

I have flopped trips (8s) and the turn 3c is a harmless blank. Claude called my large flop bet, so his continuing range is wide: single-8 hands (A8/K8/Q8), medium pairs, some two-diamond draw combos, and rarely full houses (66) or the lone 8c. I want to extract value from single-8 hands and medium pairs while denying the double-diamond draws a free river. A turn bet of about two-thirds pot applies pressure and prices in most of his calling hands without overcommitting the stack. If he calls I will re-evaluate river for further value; if he raises big I’ll narrow his range to very strong hands (full house or the lone 8) and proceed cautiously.

Minni's aggressive line (raise flop, raise turn) on a paired 8-high board strongly indicates trips or a premium hand. My Q6 gives me only a pair of 6s with approximately 15-20% equity, significantly below the 28.6% needed to justify calling 800 into a 2000 pot. While heads-up play rewards aggression, I must fold weak hands when the opponent demonstrates clear strength and my equity is insufficient. This preserves my chip stack for higher-probability opportunities where I can extract value from my position and stack advantage.

Heads-up on the button with Kd Kc — a near-premium hand. I want to raise preflop to build the pot and isolate the big blind while keeping their calling range wide. A standard 3x BB open (raise to 300 total) extracts value from worse kings, A-x, medium pairs and suited connectors, preserves post‑flop flexibility with deep stacks, and avoids overfolding strong but non-nut hands. If Claude 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and frequency (call or 4-bet for fold equity vs his tendencies); if he calls I’ll look to c-bet and extract on favorable textures. Raising to 300 maximizes expected value here.

Calling with 7♠8♠ from the Big Blind in heads-up play. This connected, suited hand has good playability with multiple ways to make strong holdings (straights, flushes). Favorable 2:1 pot odds and reasonable equity against Minni's wide raising range from Button. Standard heads-up Big Blind defense with a speculative but playable hand.

Checking with middle pair (7s with 8 kicker) on a paired 3-3 board in heads-up play. This is a medium-strength hand that benefits from seeing another card while gathering information about Minni's range. The paired flop warrants a cautious approach - checking keeps the pot manageable and allows me to respond to her action with better information. If she bets, I can evaluate her likely holdings and determine if my pair has adequate equity to continue.

Heads-up I’m the preflop raiser with KK on 3c-7d-3h — a dry, low, paired board. Claude checked, and my kings are a clean overpair to virtually his calling range (A-x, K-x, medium pairs, suited connectors). A standard continuation bet extracts value from worse pairs and K/Ax hands, charges backdoor draws, and gets folding equity against floating/weak holdings. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (300) to balance value and protection without bloating the pot versus a possible set (33) or 77. If he raises I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (call smaller, fold or proceed cautiously to large, polarized raises); if he calls I’ll proceed for value on safe turns.

Heads-up situation with 7♠8♠ on a 3♣7♦3♥ board. I have a pair of sevens with good kicker. Getting 4:1 pot odds (900+300 to call 300) which are favorable for my equity. On a paired flop, Minni's bet of 300 into 900 could represent value with a 3x, but in heads-up play where she has a wide raising range, she could also be aggressively testing with weaker holdings. My hand has several avenues: the pair is decent but vulnerable, and I have two overs and a gutshot straight draw (any 5 or 6 completes a straight). The superior odds justify a call to see the turn where I can better evaluate her holdings and my equity. Folding here would be unnecessarily exploitable in a heads-up context where aggression is common.

Betting with pair of 7s + open-ended straight draw on a safe turn card. The 5♥ is unlikely to have helped Minni significantly from her wide button range. Value-betting when ahead with strong drawing potential. Standard aggression in heads-up play against her flop continuation. Looking to build the pot, extract value from weaker hands, and maintain control of the hand. The straight draw provides excellent equity insurance if called.

Claude check‑called my flop c‑bet on 3c‑7d‑3h and now raises the 5h turn to 800. My K♦K♣ is a big overpair to most of his continuing range (A‑x, K‑x, medium pairs, floats) but is crushed by any hand containing a 3 or a 7 (trip 3s or a set) and behind some two‑pair combos that the 5 can create. The raise size is modest relative to the pot and with deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot when I’m potentially behind. Calling controls the pot, keeps his bluffs and worse hands in, and gives me a clean river decision (value‑bet small on weakness or fold to heavy polarization). Pot odds (~28.6% to break even) justify a call here. I’ll call 800 and re‑evaluate on the river.

Checking river with a pair of 7s and 8 kicker on a paired 3-3 board. This is a prudent showdown line that avoids losing additional chips if Minni holds a 3. Checking allows me to see her action and reach showdown safely with a hand that may be ahead against her wide range. Betting would be speculatively bluffing into potential strength given the board texture and her call on the turn.

Claude check‑called my flop c‑bet and then check‑raised the turn to 800 — that line is heavily weighted toward very strong made hands (trip 3s, trip 7s, turned set of 5s, and a handful of two‑pair/set combos). The river 2c is a blank relative to that range and his check is consistent with pot‑control or a small trap rather than wholesale weakness. Betting here would mostly fold out the worse hands I beat anyway and will get called or raised by the hands that dominate me; given the turn raise I expect his continuing range to contain many value combos that crush KK. The pot (2,800) is small relative to our stacks, so there's no need to manufacture fold equity by bloating a pot I'm likely behind in. I’ll check and go to showdown to avoid risking more chips when I have an overpair but a polarized opponent.

In heads-up play at hand 96 of 150, we're nearly 2/3 through the tournament with relatively even stacks (20,180 vs 19,670). K9o is above-average in heads-up range and definitely playable from the button. I should be aggressive here and apply pressure with a standard 3x raise. This hand has decent playability postflop with king-high and multiple overcards/straight draw potential. Raising to 300 is a standard aggression play that's appropriate for this stage and situation.

Claude opened to 300 from the button (SB) in heads-up. Pot is 400 and I only need to call 200 more (total 300) — ~33% pot odds. T♥4♦ is marginal but defensible heads-up against a wide SB opening, and stacks are deep so there are postflop implied odds to realize. I’m out of position, so I won’t thin‑value 3‑bet with this hand; a flat call keeps the pot manageable and lets me proceed based on the flop texture (fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, continue or raise when I pair the Ten or pick up good equity). Call to see the flop (total committed = 300).

Flop: 4c Ts 8c — my hand Th4d is top two pair (Tens and Fours) in a HU pot. Claude opened wide from the button preflop, so his continuing range contains single pairs (Tx, 8x, 4x), flush and club draws, overcards, and the occasional set. I’m out of position so checking gives free cards to draws and weaker hands that will call a bet. I want value and protection: a bet charges club flush draws and straight/overcard draws while getting called by worse pairs and some draws. Size: 400 into a 600 pot (~2/3 pot) is large enough to deny or price out many draws and extract value from single pairs and weaker hands, while still leaving room to fold/call/raise on later streets if Claude shows heavy resistance. Bet 400 and re-evaluate on his action.

In heads-up play at hand 96/150, I need to evaluate K9o on a flop of 4c-Ts-8c after Minni raises to 400. My hand has decent potential: (1) top pair possibilities with the King, (2) a gutshot straight draw with any Queen, (3) potential to hit a 9 for a pair. I'm getting 2.5:1 pot odds (1400 to 400), which is favorable given my equity. While K9 is marginal, it has approximately 27-30% equity against Minni's typical raising range in heads-up play at this stage. With stacks deep and relatively even (19,930 vs 19,070), calling is appropriate to see the turn card. Folding would be too passive in heads-up play where aggression and hand development are critical. I maintain my position while keeping multiple paths to win the hand.

I have top two pair (Tens and Fours) on 4c Ts 8c 5h. Claude called my sizable flop bet, so his continuing range is wide: worse Tens, one-pair hands (8x, 4x), occasional overcards, and the rare two-club combo draw. The 5h on the turn doesn't change my relative strength much (it does give up the unlikely possibility of trip fives), so the best plan is to extract value and deny equity: make a value/protection bet of roughly 2/3 pot to charge worse pairs and price out backdoor clubs/straight possibilities. If he calls I can re-evaluate on the river; if he check-raises large he likely has a set or very strong two-pair and I'll re-weight between calling and folding depending on sizing (call small-to-medium raises, fold to an extreme polarized shove if it puts me in a very poor spot).

In heads-up play at hand 96/150, K9o on 4c-Ts-8c-5h has solid equity approximately 30-35% against Minni's typical turn-raising range. With pot odds of 2.56:1 requiring only 28% equity, this is a profitable call. I have multiple outs: gutshot straight draw (Q), overcards (K), and run-out possibilities. With 54 hands remaining and deep stacks, calling allows me to see the river and make a final decision. Folding K9 with these odds in heads-up would be too passive; raising risks over-committing. The river is the final card, so calling maintains proper pot discipline while keeping my tournament position competitive.

I have top two pair (Tens and Fours) on 4c Ts 8c 5h 3s. Claude (button) called my flop and turn bets, which puts him in a sticky, one‑pair / draw calling range (lots of Tx, some 8x/4x, occasional club/backdoor combos). The river 3s changes very little — it only helps pocket 3s to trips (unlikely) and doesn't complete obvious straights or flushes for most of his calling range. Betting for thin value is the best line: I want to extract calls from worse Tens, single 8s/4s and other one‑pair hands while not giving free showdowns. A medium size balances value and keeps weaker hands willing to call — half‑pot is appropriate here. I will bet 1,600 into the 3,200 pot. If Claude raises, I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing (call modest raises; fold to an extreme polarized shove), but on this river the default is to press for value.

River call with K9o on 4c-Ts-8c-5h-3s against Minni's river raise. While K-high is marginal, pot odds of 25% and heads-up dynamics justify the call. Minni could be bluffing or semi-bluffing with missed draws. Folding K-high with reasonable odds is too passive in this heads-up endgame context. This maintains proper tournament discipline while keeping maximum equity realization.

Heads‑up on the button (small blind) with 7♠6♠ — a very playable suited connector in heads‑up, especially with deep stacks (≈229BB). Raising to the minimum (200 total) gives me initiative and fold equity, isolates Claude as the single opponent, and keeps the pot size reasonable for deep‑stack postflop play. Calling to 100 would be passive and cede initiative; folding is too tight. I’ll raise to 200 to play positionally and put pressure on Claude, planning to proceed cautiously if he 3‑bets large and to play aggressively on favorable flops.

Folding 2♣ 6♥ from the big blind against Minni's raise to 200. This is one of the worst starting hands in poker with minimal equity against virtually any reasonable range. Despite having 4:1 pot odds on the call, 2-6 offsuit lacks playability postflop and will rarely be ahead. My stack (16,930) is healthy enough to wait for better spots. Preserving chips with marginal hands is crucial for tournament survival in the long run.

Raising to 200 total (150 more) from the button in heads-up play. 8-4o is weak in absolute terms, but positional advantage on the button is significant in heads-up poker. Standard strategy dictates applying pressure with a wide range from the button to avoid being exploited by frequent folds. This is a min-raise that applies pressure while keeping the pot structure reasonable.

Heads-up in the big blind with J♣ T♠ against a button open to 200. JTs is a strong defending hand vs a likely wide SB opening and plays very well postflop (pair+straight/flush equity). Calling the extra 100 (200 total committed) keeps the pot manageable out of position, preserves stack depth for postflop maneuvering, and allows me to realize equity on many flops. A min-3bet to 300 would be a small, inefficient raise that just bloats the pot out of position with limited fold equity, so I’ll call and evaluate on the flop.

I missed the flop with J♣T♠ on Qc‑6h‑3s — two overcards and no meaningful draw. Claude opened wide from the button, so he’ll c‑bet this very dry board a lot, but I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with marginal equity. Best line is pot control and a float: check and induce a c‑bet. Plan: call small–medium continuation bets to realize equity and look for a favorable turn; fold to large overbets or barrels that price me out; if he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn.

Checking with 8-4o on a Q-high flop. This hand has zero made value and no meaningful draws. While c-betting is theoretically standard heads-up play, bluffing with the worst possible hand is only profitable with sufficient fold equity. Given Minni's call pre-flop and subsequent check, they likely have some piece of this board or a hand that will continue. Checking preserves chips, allows me to see another card inexpensively, and avoids bluffing with the worst hand in poker. In a 150-hand tournament at hand 98, chip preservation on marginal bluffs is strategically sound.

I’ve improved to middle pair (T) with a J kicker on the turn (Qc‑6h‑3s‑Td). Both players checked the flop, so Claude’s checked range is wide and contains many hands that are now behind my pair (weak broadways, Ax, lower pocket pairs, and some floats). Betting for value and to deny a free river is the best line out of position — I want to extract from worse tens/one‑pair hands and charge any overcards that might pick up a free card. I’ll size down to keep the pot manageable and induce calls from worse hands: bet ~½ pot (200). If he raises big I’ll seriously re‑evaluate/fold; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the river.

With 8-4o on a 6h-3s-Qc-Td board, I have the worst possible hand in poker with zero made value and no draws. Minni's turn raise after both players checked the flop indicates she has likely improved her hand or has a strong semi-bluff. While positional aggression is valuable in heads-up play, it cannot override basic hand strength and pot equity considerations. Calling 200 to win 800 requires at least 25% equity; my 8-4o has essentially 0% equity against a reasonable range from an opponent showing strength on the turn. We're at hand 98 of 150, and chip preservation against marginal spots is strategically important. Folding is the mathematically sound decision and preserves my 16,730 chips for situations where I have actual hand strength or better equity.

Heads‑up on the button with Td5c — a weak but playable hand in position. In heads‑up play I want to be aggressive and try to steal the BB; my standard open size at this table has been 300. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) puts pressure on Claude, preserves initiative postflop, and lets me play the hand in position. If Claude 3‑bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll c‑bet favorable flops and control pot size with a marginal holding. Total commit after this action will be 300 chips (I’ve already posted 50, so I add 250).

Calling with K-2d in heads-up Big Blind. The 3:1 pot odds justify the call, and in heads-up play we need to defend the blind with a wide range. Although out of position post-flop, we have playability with a king and decent drawing potential. The hand strength is reasonable for heads-up dynamics where many hands are weak.

Checking K-2d on A-8-3 flop in heads-up play after Minni checked. This is a weak hand (K-high with poor kicker) that doesn't warrant a bet. The ace on board is concerning, and my king is likely behind Minni's range. Checking keeps the pot small, lets me control investment with a marginal hand, and allows me to see more cards cheaply. Minni's pre-flop check suggests weakness, and I can win uncontested if a king comes or continue defending passively.

Heads‑up in position with T5o on 3h-8s-Ac. I was the preflop opener and Claude checked, so a continuation bet here has good fold equity and keeps initiative. I have no pair but a half‑pot c‑bet (~50%) credibly represents Ax or broadways and will fold many hands that called preflop (small pairs, weaker broadways, floats). Stacks are deep so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal holding; this size balances fold equity and pot control. Plan: bet 300 to take the pot now or see a cheap turn; if Claude raises to a large size I’ll give up, if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn.

Folding K-2o on Ac-8s-3h board. This is an extremely weak hand with poor equity (~20-25%) against Minni's range, despite the 3:1 pot odds putting me near break-even. The ace on board is concerning, my kicker is terrible, and I'm out of position for remaining streets. This is the type of marginal hand where I should maintain discipline and avoid bloating the pot. Preserving chips in marginal spots with weak holdings is crucial in tournament poker.

Raising with pocket deuces on the button in heads-up play. This is a standard aggressive line that applies pressure early. The minimum raise to 200 is efficient given the position advantage and reasonable equity this hand has against Minni's wide big blind range. Even if called, pocket deuces has decent showdown value and can improve on the flop.

Heads-up vs the button's min-open to 200 and I'm in the BB with 4♠5♥. Calling the extra 100 (200 total committed preflop) is cheap given the pot (≈3:1 pot odds) and this hand has reasonable playability heads-up with deep stacks — straights/two-pair possibilities and useful showdown equity. I'm out of position postflop, so I don't want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand by raising; I'll call to see a flop and fold to heavy aggression if I miss. Call 100 more (200 total).

I completely missed this flop with 4♠5♥ on 9♣-6♥-J♣: no pair, no flush draw and only a very remote runner-runner straight possibility. Pot is small (400) and I’m out of position versus a wide button opener, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, lets me see what Claude does, and allows me to fold to meaningful pressure while calling small c-bets for pot odds to realize my tiny backdoor equity. I won’t bluff-raise or lead here — check and reevaluate on the turn.

Continuation betting on the flop after Minni's check. Standard heads-up play dictates aggressive follow-up when opponent shows weakness. While deuces are weak on a 9-6-J board, the check-to-me dynamic makes this an excellent opportunity to win the pot immediately or define my hand strength. A 250 c-bet (roughly 60% pot) applies sufficient pressure without overcommitting. This is a crucial aggressive play in heads-up poker.

Out of position with 4♠5♥ on 9♣-6♥-J♣. I checked the flop to control the pot and see what Claude does; his 250 into a 650 pot is a standard, relatively small c-bet. Calling 250 gives me ~27.8% pot odds (250/(650+250)). My realistic equity to make at least a pair by the river is roughly ~24% plus tiny runner-runner backdoor equity — borderline but close enough given the price. Stacks are deep so calling is cheap and keeps me in against a wide button betting range; raising OOP with no pair or meaningful draw would be irresponsible. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve, and only continue as a bluff in very specific, controlled spots. Total committed = 200 (preflop) + 250 (call) = 450.

Out of position with 4♠5♥ on 9♣-6♥-J♣-3♠. I have no made hand — only 10 realistic outs (three 4s, three 5s, four 7s) ≈ 10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve by the river. Pot is 900 and there is currently no bet to call on the turn, so I should control the pot and realize my equity rather than bluff or inflate OOP. Leading or raising here with no pair and weak blockers is a mistake against a wide/button opening range. Plan: check and see the river for free; if Claude bets the river I’ll call small bets (rough break-even is about a 250-chip river bet given my ~22% equity) and fold to larger sizing. Check to preserve stack and keep options for the river.

Checking the turn with pocket deuces on a 9-6-J-3 board. My hand is extremely weak with no improvement and minimal showdown value. Minni has checked twice, suggesting weakness, but betting with this hand is too risky—if he raises, I'm forced to fold and lose additional chips. Checking allows me to see the river for free, preserving my stack for better opportunities while keeping the option to bluff or value bet depending on the river card and board dynamics.

Board: 9c 6h Jc 3s 9h. I hold 4s5h — no pair, no meaningful draw, and I have poor blockers to the pair (9) or J. Preflop Claude opened and c-bet the flop ~250, I called; both checked the turn. Pot is 900 and there's currently no river bet. Bluffing here has little fold equity: Claude checking the turn suggests a wide range that contains many hands that will call a river bluff (Jx, pocket pairs, missed overcards) and he now benefits from the paired board — trips/full house are possible. I also have essentially zero showdown value, so calling a bet is not an option unless it's an indeterminate tiny probe. The least costly, highest EV line is to check and see a showdown for free: this preserves my stack, avoids getting exploited by a river check-raise or a thin value bet, and only loses the pot when I’m beaten (very likely). Alternative (small blocker bluff) is inferior because I have weak blockers and Claude has deep stacks to call. Action: check.

River decision with pocket deuces on a 9-6-J-3-9 board. My hand strength is extremely weak—I have pocket deuces but the board shows a pair of nines, so my best hand is essentially the board pair (9-9-J-6-3) with my 2 as kicker. Minni's line of checking twice (turn and river) indicates weakness or a marginal hand, but after he called my flop c-bet, he almost certainly has at least one pair or multiple overcards. A bluff bet here is unlikely to be profitable—if called, I lose; if folded to, Minni was bluffing which seems unlikely given his calling pattern. Checking allows me to see the hand at showdown with zero additional risk, and there's a small chance Minni has complete air. This preserves my stack (15,980) for better opportunities ahead in the tournament, especially given we're only at hand 100 of 150. The conservative line is the mathematically sound play here with such a weak holding.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Q♦5♣ — a marginal hand but perfectly reasonable to open for initiative. I want to play last postflop and apply pressure to Claude’s blind rather than limp in and give him cheap odds to see a flop. My stack (23k) is deep relative to the blinds, so a standard 3xBB open is a small, low-risk way to steal the blind and define the hand. Folding is too nitty and calling is passive; raise to 300 to take the initiative and keep the pot manageable.

Folding 69o from the big blind against Minni's button raise. While the hand has marginal equity against a wide range, being out of position with a weak, unconnected hand against an aggressive opponent creates unfavorable post-flop situations. With a solid stack and 50 hands remaining, I should wait for better opportunities rather than playing weak hands out of position. This is a standard fold.

In heads-up play on the button, positional advantage is critical. Q3o is a marginal hand, but it's well within my opening range from the button (SB) against Minni in the big blind. 

Looking at the tournament dynamics:
- We're at hand 102 of 150, past midway
- Stacks are relatively balanced (16,730 vs 23,120)
- Heads-up poker demands wide opening ranges from the button
- Q3o is definitely a raising hand in this spot

I should apply standard heads-up strategy: raise to around 2.5-3x the big blind. A raise to 250 total (150 more) gives good fold equity while maintaining reasonable sizing. This is a standard play that puts pressure on Minni and lets me take the initiative post-flop with position.

Heads-up vs Claude (button) who opened to 250. I have 7♣6♣ in the big blind — a suited connector with good flop-play and equity against a wide opening range. Pot is 350 and I must call 150 more (my total commitment will be 250), which gives me ~30% required equity to continue. With deep stacks and a wide SB opening range, 7♣6♣ has enough equity and implied odds to call. I’m out of position postflop, so I’d rather keep the pot controllable and see a flop instead of bloating the pot with a marginal 3‑bet. Plan: see flop, continue with pairs/draws, fold to heavy turn/river pressure without improvement, or apply aggression if the board and reads allow.

Check. I limped to see a flop with 7c6c and the board 8h-4c-3h gives me only a 4‑out straight draw to any 5 (≈8.5% to hit on the turn, ≈17% by the river) plus a small backdoor club flush (~4%). I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so I want pot control and to realize equity rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Checking keeps options open: call small turn bets to continue with my draws, fold to heavy pressure if I miss, or take a free card if Claude checks behind. Betting here would commit me too often with too little equity, so I check.

Standard heads-up continuation bet from position. My pair of threes is likely ahead of Minni's checking range. A 300 bet (60% of the 500 pot) applies appropriate pressure without overcommitting. In heads-up play with position after raising pre-flop, I should be continuation betting most flops, especially when my opponent shows weakness with a check.

Call 300. Claude's small-flop bet (300 into 800) gives me ~27% pot-equity requirement (300 to win ~1100). With 7♣6♣ on 8♥-4♣-3♥ I have a 4‑out gutshot to a 5 (≈8.5% turn, ≈17% by river) plus 6 pair‑outs; overall chance to improve to at least a pair/straight by the river is roughly ~38%, so this is +EV vs Claude's wide heads‑up range. I'm out of position and stacks are deep, so I want to keep the pot manageable and realize equity rather than turning this into a marginal bluff/raising war. Plan: call to see the turn; if I hit a 5 (straight) or pair (6/7) play for value; if I miss and face heavy turn/river pressure I will fold; I can call smaller turn barrels to realize equity.

Board: 8♥ 4♣ 3♥ J♣. I hold 7♣6♣ — a four-card club and two pair/straight outs (9 club outs + 3 sixes + 3 sevens = 15 outs ≈ 33% to improve on the river). Claude checked the turn, showing weakness or pot control out of a wide heads‑up range. With pot = 1,100 I want to apply pressure as a semi‑bluff: a modest bet steals many unpaired hands and weak pairs while keeping the price reasonable if called. A bet of 400 (~36% pot) has a break‑even fold frequency of ~27% and, even when called, I retain ~33% equity to improve. Plan: bet 400 to take the initiative; if Claude folds I win the pot; if he calls I proceed to the river (value bet when I hit a club or pair, fold to heavy river pressure if I miss). If he check‑raises large OOP I’ll concede and fold — don’t get sticky with a marginal hand out of position.

Folding pair of threes to a turn check-raise. My bottom pair lacks equity against Minni's aggressive action, which strongly suggests a stronger holding (likely a pair). The Jc on the turn could have improved her hand significantly. With only 20% needed equity at 3.75:1 odds but likely having 10-15% at best, this is a straightforward fold. Position disadvantage on the river makes this decision even clearer.

Heads-up on the button with A♥2♣ — this is a very playable hand in position. Limping/calling 50 is too passive heads-up and gives the BB free opportunity to take the initiative or isolate; raising is standard. I'll raise to the minimum allowed (200 total) — a 2x sizing keeps the pot manageable, applies pressure to Claude's wide BB defending range, and gives me the advantage of acting last postflop. I keep options to fold to a big 3-bet or flop-play aggressively with position and an ace-blocker; overall this maximizes short-term fold equity and long-term EV from this spot.

Heads-up play requires aggressive big blind defense. Q-3 is marginal but playable given 3:1 pot odds and the standard raise size. I have position post-flop and can make decisions based on the flop texture. Folding too much in this spot is exploitable long-term.

I hold Q♥-3♠ on a Q♠-8♦-5♦ flop in heads-up play. I have top pair but with a weak kicker - a significant vulnerability. The pot is 400 with deep effective stacks (I have ~16k, Minni has ~23.5k). By checking, I accomplish several things: (1) I keep the pot small with a marginal hand that loses to better queens and draws; (2) I encourage Minni to continuation bet, which allows me to gather information about their holding; (3) I retain the option to call, raise, or fold based on their action; (4) A weak-kicker top pair plays better in smaller pots. Minni raised pre-flop from the button with a wide range, so they'll likely c-bet many hands. I can then make an informed decision. Checking also prevents me from inflating the pot when I'm vulnerable, which is sound bankroll management in heads-up play.

Heads-up on the button with A♥2♣. Claude checked the dry-ish Q♠8♦5♦ flop — this is a great spot to c-bet to take the initiative. I have an ace-blocker and decent fold equity against a wide BB defending range; the board has two diamonds so I’ll avoid an over-sized stab but I want to apply pressure to weak pairs, missed broadways and pure floats. A half-pot continuation bet (~200 into the 400 pot) balances fold equity and protection, lets me realize equity cheaply if called, and keeps options to barrel or pot-control on later streets. If Claude raises big I'll fold and preserve my stack; if he calls I'll re-evaluate on the turn.

Facing a 200 bet into a 600 pot on the flop with Q♥-3♠ against Q♠-8♦-5♦. I'm getting 3:1 odds to call. Despite having a weak-kicker top pair, this is a profitable spot:

1. Pot odds are favorable: I'm risking 200 to win 800 (3:1 return)
2. My hand has reasonable equity: Top pair beats all undercards and worse queens; my main vulnerabilities are better queens and sets, but these aren't the entire range
3. Position and information: By calling and seeing the turn, I gather more information about Minni's hand strength. If a blank comes, I can make a more informed decision. If a favorable card comes, I can continue
4. Stack depth: We're still deep (effective stack ~16k, pot ~800), so I'm not committed
5. Heads-up dynamics: Minni is applying a modest bet (half-pot) which suggests either a c-bet or medium strength hand. Calling keeps the option to evaluate further rather than folding too much top pair in heads-up play

This balanced approach keeps the pot manageable while maintaining equity with top pair.

Checking the turn with Q♥-3♠ on Q♠-8♦-5♦-5♥. My top pair is weak-kicker vulnerable on a paired board. Minni's turn check suggests moderate strength or a draw. By checking, I: (1) take a free river card without risking additional chips with marginal holding; (2) maintain position and flexibility for river decision-making; (3) avoid inflating the pot with a hand that's often behind better queens or sets; (4) gather information about Minni's hand strength on the river action. Deep stacks allow me to play this cautiously. Heads-up dynamics favor keeping pot small with marginal hands until I have complete board information.

I raised preflop and c-bet the dry Q♠8♦5♦ flop (I have A♥2♣) and Claude called. The turn pairs the board (5♥) which significantly increases the likelihood that any caller who held a 5 or a Q (or a pocket pair) has improved to a very strong holding (trips/full-house or two pair). I only have ace-high with no diamond backdoor, so my showdown equity is weak and my fold equity on a turn bluff is limited — Claude has shown a tendency to be sticky and to call flop bets. With pot control and stack preservation as priorities (pot = 800, stacks deep), I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal double-barrel that will get called enough to be unprofitable. I’ll check and evaluate the river: if Claude bluffs a small river bet I can call; if he fires a large bet and I haven’t improved I’ll fold; if he checks we go to a cheap showdown.

River check with Q♥-3♠ on Q♠-8♦-5♦-5♥-4♥. Minni's double-barrel checking (turn and river) indicates clear weakness or missed draw. My weak-kicker top pair is vulnerable to better queens and any underpairs that slow-played. Betting risks additional chips with a marginal hand; checking guarantees winning the 800-chip pot with likely the best hand. Tournament position (47 hands remaining) favors conservative chip preservation in thin spots. Free showdown is optimal.

I c-bet the Q♠8♦5♦ flop and Claude called; the turn pairing the board to 5♥ massively reduces my fold equity and improves a lot of Claude's calling hands (any 5, some pocket pairs, and hands that picked up backdoor equity). I checked the turn to control the pot — with A♥2♣ I have only ace-high and no diamond backdoor, so I don't want to inflate the pot with a marginal bluff. Claude checked the river as well (Q♠8♦5♦5♥4♥). Betting here would be a pure bluff against a player who has already shown a willingness to call flop bets; many of Claude's hands that beat me (5x, pocket pairs) will still call, so a river bluff has limited fold equity. With deep stacks relative to the 800 pot and many hands left in the tournament, preserving equity and taking a cheap showdown is the better line. I’ll check and call a small river probe if he leads, but I won’t bluff the river into a player who’s shown stickiness.

Heads-up play with AT (Ace-Ten). I'm on the button/small blind with good odds - only need to call 50 more chips. AT is a strong hand in heads-up dynamics and should be played aggressively. I'm chip-aware (opponent has 23,270 vs my 16,580), so I need to build pots with premium hands. A raise here applies pressure, takes down the blind frequently, or builds the pot when called. Standard 2.5-3x big blind raise is appropriate. Raising to 300 total (250 additional) puts meaningful pressure on the big blind while controlling pot size.

Facing a standard button raise to 300 while I'm in the big blind with 6♣2♥. This is one of the worst starting hands: unsuited, unconnected and heavily dominated by the raiser’s likely button range. Calling 200 for a pot that effectively requires ~33% equity to break even is unattractive — my equity vs a typical open range is well below that and I’d be OOP for the rest of the hand. A small 3-bet here is a poor bluff (no useful blockers) and opens a difficult out-of-position fight with a deeper stack. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I’m on the button (SB) with J♠4♠ — a marginal but suited hand. In heads-up play I want to take the initiative rather than limp; calling 50 is passive and a min-raise to 200 is too small and just invites 3-bets. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x the BB) to steal the blind and get fold equity while keeping the pot controllable with a weak holding. If Claude 3-bets large I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play cautiously on wet or heavily overcarded boards.

Calling with KcTc from BB. Minni is a button aggressor with a wide range. KT suited has strong playability - can flop pairs, broadway, draws. 2:1 pot odds (400:200) justify the call. With 16,630 chips (166 BBs), I have room to navigate post-flop and can outplay Minni's frequent aggression from position.

Betting KT of clubs on 9-3-8. Strong semi-bluff with multiple outs (gutshot straight, two overcards, two flush cards). Minni's check from SB suggests I can take down the pot now or build value. Half-pot bet (300) maintains fold equity while keeping stack ratios favorable. This hand has excellent playability regardless of turn outcome.

Call. The flop (9♦ 3♠ 8♥) is very dry and my J♠4♠ has only one real piece of equity — an overcard (J) and a backdoor spade possibility that can turn into a real draw. Claude’s 300 is a small-to-medium c-bet out of the BB who called my preflop raise, so his range is wide (top pairs, middling pairs, some floats and air). Pot odds (call 300 to win ~1200) require only ~20% equity, which J4s can achieve enough of versus a broad c-bet range. Raising here would be building a pot with very little fold equity and lots of hands that currently crush me. I’ll call 300 to see the turn, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate: fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, bet/raise if I pick up a J or a favorable runout (or if he shows weakness), and use position to apply pressure on a checked turn.

Check on the turn with K-high. The board pair of 8s is unfavorable for my range, I have no straight or flush draws, and I'm out of position facing an opponent who called my flop bet. With deep stacks (161 BBs), folding weak holdings is profitable. I'll evaluate the river and either make a thin value bet with my overcards or check-fold depending on what comes and Minni's action.

I have J♠4♠ on 9♦ 3♠ 8♥ 8♠ — I’m sitting on a strong turn semi‑bluff: a four‑flush with 9 spade outs plus three jacks (roughly 12 outs total, ~26% to improve to at least a pair/flush on the river). Claude led the flop (300) and then checked the turn — that line widens his range (could be floats, medium pairs, or some pot‑control hands) and suggests we can fold out a lot of his weaker holdings by taking the initiative. The pot is 1,200; a bet of ~2/3 pot applies meaningful fold equity while not overcommitting if I get raised. Betting 800 forces Claude to call 800 to win ~2,800 (he needs ~28.6% equity), which folds many Kx/Qx/air combos and lets me build a pot when I hit. Plan: bet 800 as a semi‑bluff; if he raises big I’ll respect it and fold (he’s likely made trips/full house); if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river — push for value when I hit the flush, otherwise check/fold to sizeable aggression.

Folding K-high on paired board without any draws against a turn raise. I had a legitimate semi-bluff on the flop with two flush cards and a gutshot, but the 8s on the turn gave me no equity improvement. K-high plays poorly out of position against Minni's raising range. Even with 3.5:1 pot odds, my estimated equity of 25-28% is at or below break-even. No draws means no implied odds to justify the call. With 161 BBs, folding a marginal spot is optimal play.

Raising with 8s Qd from the button in heads-up play. This is a standard 3x big blind raise. Queen-high is a solid hand for button raises in heads-up situations, and the raise builds the pot while maintaining positional advantage post-flop. The raise is consistent with my recent aggression from the button.

I’m in the big blind with Qh3c facing a button open to 300 (pot 400, 200 to call). Q3o is a marginal hand but heads-up vs a wide button opening I should defend. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~33% pot odds and the stacks are deep, so calling to realize equity is the low-variance, correct play. 3-betting OOP with this hand is too thin (and would leave me out of position postflop); folding is too nitty given the cheap price. Plan: call to see the flop, continue aggressively if I pair the Q or pick up strong equity, and concede to large pressure on dry unfavorable flops.

I have Qh3c on Ks-6s-3d — bottom pair (a deuce/trey pair) with a decent kicker but I’m out of position. Claude’s button open is wide and includes lots of Kx and overcards that currently dominate me, so there’s no reason to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity and concede to heavy pressure. Plan: check and call small-to-medium continuation bets (to ~1/2 pot) to see further streets, but fold to large raises that strongly represent two-pair/sets or a king.

With 8s Qd on a flop of 6s Ks 3d, I have only Queen-high with no significant draws. Minni checked from the big blind, indicating weakness. Given my marginal holding and lack of equity against most of Minni's likely range, checking is the prudent play. This allows me to see the turn cheaply and avoid burning chips with a weak hand. I can reassess on the turn if the board improves or if Minni shows further weakness. Betting here with Q-high would be speculative and expose me to further aggression with limited fold equity, especially given my shorter stack (15,830).

Turn: I’ve turned trips with Qh-3c on 6s Ks 3d 3s — a very strong hand heads-up. Claude’s a wide button opener so his range has lots of Kx, broadways, suited spades and medium pairs. The turn completed a third spade, so some of his combos that included two spades have become a made flush; there are also rare K–3/full-house combos, but those are infrequent. Checking hands this spot surrenders value and gives a free river to bluffs or converts marginal hands into two-pair/full houses cheaply. I want to extract value from Kx, Ax/Qx, and medium pairs while charging any hands that now have a spade flush — but I also don’t want to bloat the pot massively versus the small set of full-house/very strong combos. A value/protection bet of roughly 2/3 pot (400 into 600) accomplishes that: it’s big enough to charge spade-made hands and get calls from worse, yet small enough relative to stacks to keep the pot manageable if Claude shows strength. Plan: if he calls I’ll reevaluate the river and continue extracting; if he raises large, I’ll give strong weight to a flush/full-house and fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing and reads.

Folding with Q-high and a marginal flush draw. While I have 9 spade outs, my total equity (~26-27%) falls slightly short of the 28.6% needed for break-even pot odds on this 400 chip call. The paired board dramatically reduces my showdown value, and my short stack (15,830) makes preserving chips more valuable than chasing marginal equity situations. Minni's turn raise after a flop check suggests a legitimate hand, likely another draw or premium holding. Better spots will come in heads-up play.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 2♣4♥ — a very weak hand. In heads-up play the button should open a very wide range to generate fold equity and leverage positional advantage. Folding is too tight given the stacks and the pot; simply calling 50 to limp in is overly passive and surrenders initiative. I’ll open-raise to a standard table sizing (300 total) — big enough to pick up decent fold equity but not commit an excessive portion of my deep stack. Plan: if Claude folds I take the blinds; if he calls I’ll c-bet on many dry/face-up boards and check/control on connected/low boards that likely hit his calling range. If Claude 3-bets to a substantial size, I will fold this hand and preserve my stack. Raise to 300 keeps the initiative and maximizes long-run EV in heads-up play.

I'm in the big blind with Ad7d against Minni's button raise to 300. This is a reasonably strong hand in heads-up play—I have an ace (highest card) with suited diamonds giving me backdoor flush potential. The pot is 400 and I'm getting 2:1 odds to call 200 more. Minni has been raising frequently from the button throughout the session with a wide range of hands, so this could be a wide range. My stack is healthy at 15,730, and I'll have position on subsequent streets post-flop. I'll call and evaluate the flop action. A 3-bet here is premature without a stronger hand like a premium pair or AK.

With Ad7d on a paired board (Th 3s Td), I have ace-high with minimal current equity and only backdoor flush draw potential. Minni's button raise could represent a very wide range in heads-up play—everything from premium pairs to weak Broadway cards. On this dry, paired flop, my ace is vulnerable and doesn't represent much strength. I'm out of position (first to act), so checking is the prudent choice. This allows me to: (1) gather information about Minni's hand strength based on her action, (2) control pot size while I'm uncertain, and (3) potentially see additional cards before committing more chips. If she bets, I can evaluate her bet size and decide whether to call, fold, or raise. If she checks back, I'll reassess on the turn with more information.

I opened wide preflop but on this flop (Th 3s Td) my 2♥4♣ is complete air. A paired ten-board heavily favors caller’s range (pocket pairs, two-pair/trips with 3s or tens, and strong broadways), and Claude’s check can be weakness or a trap. Betting here would be a pure bluff that’s easily check‑raised or called by lots of hands that already beat me. With deep stacks I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot with no equity. Plan: check and concede the initiative; fold to significant aggression on this street and re-evaluate on the turn only if a very safe card appears or Claude shows clear weakness (checks again).

Checking turn with Ad7d (ace-high only) on Th 3s Td Js board. Minni checked the flop showing weakness, but my hand has zero equity and no realistic draw improvements. Betting would be a pure bluff with a hand too weak to justify the risk. Checking allows me to see the river cheaply while managing my chip stack efficiently. If Minni bets, I can fold and preserve chips. If she checks, I have showdown value with ace-high.

I opened extremely wide preflop with 2♥4♣ to seize initiative heads‑up, but this board (Th 3s Td Js) is terrible for my range and I have no showdown equity. I intentionally checked the flop to control the pot — the double‑ten paired flop heavily favors Claude’s calling range (pocket pairs, single tens, Jx, slowplays). His check on the turn is ambiguous (could be weakness or a slowplay/trap), but leading out here would be a pure bluff that’s called or check‑raised by many hands that already beat me. With a 600 chip pot and deep stacks, I should avoid bloating the pot with air. I’ll check again, keep the pot small, and reevaluate on the river — folding to meaningful aggression unless a very favorable river arrives or Claude shows clear weakness.

River check with Ad7d (pair of tens) on Th 3s Td Js 6d. Minni has shown weakness by checking flop and turn on a relatively dry, paired board. My weak pair needs showdown value rather than bluff equity. Betting the river after she's checked twice won't win many pots through aggression, and marginal value hands are more efficiently played to showdown out of position. This preserves chips and allows me to see her hand while controlling risk with a vulnerable holding.

I opened extremely wide preflop but this river (Th 3s Td Js 6d) gives me absolutely no showdown equity with 2♥4♣. Claude’s passive line (checks flop, turn and river) is ambiguous — could be weakness but also a trap with tens/trips/Jx — and many of his likely calling hands will call a river bluff. Betting here would be a thin, high-variance bluff that’s unlikely to fold enough better hands to be +EV. I’ll keep the pot small and check to showdown; if Claude now bets I’ll fold to meaningful aggression since I have no showdown value.

In heads-up play on the button with J5o, I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (150 pot, 50 to call). While J5 is a weak hand, I have positional advantage as the small blind, which provides significant edge post-flop. I can see the flop cheaply and use my position to make decisions based on Minni's actions. Folding here gives up my small blind without gaining information, while calling allows me to play profitably with position. This is a balanced approach in deep heads-up poker where positional advantage is critical.

I’m in the big blind with 2♦6♠ and the SB (Claude) just limped. There’s no raise, pot is 200 and the min-raise is to 200. This hand has very poor equity and flop playability; against a limp/callmer who often continues postflop, a small preflop raise will usually be called and I’ll play out of position with garbage. I’ll check to see a cheap flop, keep the pot small, and fold to any sizable continuation bet on most boards. I’ll reserve preflop raises for better hands or specific bluff spots where I have blockers or clear fold equity.

I have 2♦6♠ on K♣9♥8♣ — no pair, no club, no real backdoor. Claude simply limped preflop so his range contains many pairs and hands that will continue; bluffing OOP into a single limper on this two‑club board without blockers is poor. Pot is small (200) and I should keep the pot under control and avoid tough decisions out of position. I’ll check and fold to a meaningful bet; if he checks back I get a free turn to try to improve or reassess.

On the flop with J5o against Minni's check, I have a gutshot straight draw (four tens) with no made hand. This is a marginal holding with approximately 8 outs to the straight. Since Minni checked showing weakness, I should take the free card to the turn. My hand doesn't have enough equity to profitably bet as a bluff, and checking allows me to: (1) see the turn for free, (2) potentially hit my straight draw, and (3) avoid committing chips unnecessarily with a weak holding. In heads-up deep stack play, taking free cards with draws is a fundamental principle. If I improve on the turn, I can then apply pressure.

I have 2♦6♠ on K♣9♥8♣–Q♥. Preflop Claude limped and both checked the flop, so his range is wide but contains many Kx/9x/8x and Qx combinations that the turn improved. I have no pair, no club/heart draw and no meaningful backdoors—this is pure air. Pot is small (200) and stacks are deep, so I should keep the pot under control rather than building it with a bluff that has poor blocker value and low fold equity. Action: check and plan to fold to any meaningful turn bet (maybe call a very tiny probe), and if he checks back re-evaluate on the river.

With J5o on K-9-8-Q, I have 8 straight outs (broadway and wheel possibilities) and roughly 32% equity to the river. Minni's turn check indicates weakness. In heads-up play with position and strong fold equity, betting is superior to checking the free card. A bet of 150 (75% of pot) applies pressure that's sized to both build fold equity against weak holdings and maintain value against hands I have equity against. The deep stacks support aggressive application of position with drawing hands.

Fold. I checked the turn as planned and Claude’s 150 into the ~200 pot (pot now 350) is a meaningful bet. Calling 150 for this pot requires roughly ~30% equity, and with 2♦6♠ on K♣‑9♥‑8♣‑Q♥ I have virtually no showdown or improvement equity (no pair, no flush/heart backdoor, no meaningful straight outs). Raising OOP here as a bluff has poor blocker value and low fold equity against a player who limped and now fires the turn. Preserving a 23.8k stack and waiting for a better spot or position is the right play — fold and move on.

Heads-up on the button with Qc8d — a playable hand that does well in position. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but limp-calling gives the BB cheap odds and forfeits initiative. I’ll open-raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to gain fold equity, isolate Claude and build a pot I can play from position. With deep stacks (~237BB vs ~161BB) this size leaves plenty of postflop room. Plan: continuation‑bet many flops and apply pressure; fold to significant 3‑bet aggression, but if called use position to realize equity and extract value.

J4o is a very weak hand with poor equity against Minni's button raising range. Even though the immediate pot odds are 2:1 (needing to call 200 to compete for 400), my hand only has approximately 25-30% equity against Minni's likely holdings. I'm out of position postflop, which further disadvantages this marginal hand. With 41 hands remaining in the tournament and a decent stack size (16130), folding weak unprofitable situations is the correct long-term strategy. Heads-up on the button limp/call gives Claude a very wide, often weak range (small pairs, 7x/4x, broadways, suited connectors, some two-club combos). I have top pair (9♦) but a very weak 5 kicker. Bottom pair with weak 8 kicker against a flop raise from an aggressive opponent. With 40 hands remaining and balanced stacks, this hand lacks sufficient equity to justify continuing. Folding 4-10 offsuit from the button in heads-up. While position is favorable, this is a weak, unconnected hand and I'll be out of position postflop. Heads-up on the button with A♣4♠ — this is a standard raise-first-in spot. I have position and an ace, so I want to seize the initiative, pick up the blinds, and define ranges. Calling K8o in heads-up from the big blind. Minni's 200 raise is standard, and I'm getting 3:1 odds. This hand has reasonable equity and plays well post-flop. I have K♥8♣ on a Q♦K♦3♠ flop, giving me top pair with a weak kicker in heads-up play. Call 250. Heads-up on the button with A♣4♠: I have position and a live Ace but no diamond. Claude’s flop bet (250 into the current pot) is a fairly standard c-bet on K♦ Q♦ 3♠ and will contain a mix of value (Kx/Qx/pairs) and lots of floats/flush-draws. Raising to 500 is possible as a bluff, but it bloats the pot against many hands that will call (K/Q, diamond draws) and commits me without a made hand; I prefer to use position to extract or fold on later streets. Plan: call, see the turn, and evaluate — if I pick up an Ace or a favorable blank I can take the initiative; if Claude barrels large on a wet turn without improvement, I can fold and preserve chips.

The ace on the turn is problematic for K8. It likely completes much of Minni's range (AK, AQ, AA), and my hand has diminished significantly in value. Turn: I paired my Ace (A♣4♠) on Q♦ K♦ 3♠ A♥. Claude bet the flop and then checked the turn — a line that often represents Kx/Qx, floats or missed diamond draws rather than a very strong two‑pair/trap. The ace on the turn is devastating to K8 weak kicker. Minni's action pattern (flop call → turn raise after ace comes) strongly indicates she has an ace (AK, AQ, AA) or flopped a strong hand. Heads-up vs a 2BB open from the button. 9♣7♣ is a playable suited connector with good flop playability and implied odds against a wide opening range. Out of position on a K♠ J♠ 2♦ flop with 9♣7♣. I have no pair, no spade backdoor and only very remote straight chances. I have Tc Kh and flopped top pair on a K-2-J board. Minni checked, which is a soft action after I raised pre-flop. Small c-bet (250 into 650 ≈ 38% pot). Calling 250 to win 900 gives ~28% pot odds. I just paired the 7 on the turn (7d) giving me second pair (7c7d). Claude opened wide preflop and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn — showing some weakness or a missed spade/semi-bluff. Calling with top pair (Kh-Tc) on a coordinated board (2d Ks Js 7d). While Minni's turn raise is concerning, folding top pair in heads-up play is too weak, especially when chip-disadvantaged. I have second pair (7s) on a K♠ J♠ 2♦ 7♦ 4♦ board. Betting top pair on the river after Minni checks. Her turn raise followed by river check suggests potential weakness (semi-bluff that missed or medium-strength hand checking showdown value). Heads-up on the river my 9♣7♣ makes second pair (pair of 7s) after I bet the turn and Claude called. He now fires a small probe (400 into a 2200 pot) after I checked. Calling is mathematically sound with 4:1 pot odds and ~39% equity from my 8-out straight draw. With only 9-high and just 6 outs to a pair on the river (~13% equity), betting would be a fundamental error. Minni's turn check indicates weakness but doesn't help my hand. Check. I have an underpair (22) on A–K–4–Q after my flop c-bet was called. River check with 9-high on a premium board (AKQJx). I have no made hand after my straight draw missed. Heads-up button play with Q7o. Minni is in the big blind. With 29 hands remaining and relatively balanced stacks, I should leverage my positional advantage. C-bet the flop with a standard bet of 70% pot. Minni's check indicates weakness, and aggressive play is critical in heads-up when behind in chips. Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with J♦7♠ — a marginal but perfectly playable hand in heads-up. I'm in the Big Blind heads-up against Minni who raised to 200 from the Button/SB. My hand is 3h-5h, which is one of the weakest hands in poker - essentially playing 3-5 offsuit.

Check. I’m OOP with 3h6h on a very dry 9s‑2h‑4c flop — no pair but backdoor heart/straight potential (a 5 on the turn completes 2‑3‑4‑5‑6; two hearts on turn+river complete a flush).
