Claude's flop bet is small (250 into a 850 pot) on a very dry 9♠‑2♥‑4♣ board. I hold 3♥6♥ — no pair but backdoor heart potential and realistic turn-improvement outs. Calling 250 (550 total committed including my preflop 300) gives me about 22.7% break‑even equity (250 to win ~1100). I have ~10 outs to improve on the turn (three 3s + three 6s + four 5s = 10 outs ≈ 21% to hit on the turn) plus backdoor flush possibilities and additional river equity, and Claude's heads‑up c‑bet range is wide. Raising OOP with this weak, non‑made hand is too thin and bloats the pot; folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to large turn aggression if I don't improve, and play aggressively if I pick up a heart/5/pair or if Claude checks the turn.

Turn card (5♥) completed a made straight: with 3♥6♥ I now have 2-3-4-5-6. This is a very strong, likely-best hand heads-up — the only real turn/river dangers are a river heart giving a flush to a two-heart holding or a river pairing the board when Claude already holds a pair (full house). I don’t want to check and give free cards to those draws. I should bet for value and protection to charge heart/straight draws and to get called by worse hands (pairs, single heart + overcards, bluffs). Size to about 2/3 pot so I get value without folding out too many callers: pot is 1100, so I’ll bet 700. If Claude check-raises big I’ll re-evaluate (calling is reasonable given my equity vs his wide raising range), and on a heart or paired river I’ll play the showdown carefully.

The turn 5 brought no help to JT. I have zero straight outs (would need two-card combinations) and only ~6 overcard outs for thin pairs, giving me approximately 18-22% equity. Minni's turn raise indicates strength—likely a made hand. With required equity of ~27% to justify the 700 call against a 2500 total pot, and my actual equity being 5+ points short, this is a clear fold. In heads-up play, while aggression matters, it must be paired with hand strength or solid equity. Folding preserves chips for better spots in the remaining 26 hands.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 7h5c. In heads-up play the opening range from the button is very wide — 7‑5o is marginal in full-ring but perfectly reasonable to open here to take initiative and use positional advantage postflop. Limping hands lets the BB see cheap flops and surrenders fold equity; a standard 2.5–3x open is preferable. I’ll raise to 300 (a standard 3x-style open at this table) to build the pot when I’m likely ahead, deny free equity, and keep pressure on Claude. If he calls I’ll use position to c‑bet favorable, low/connected flops and play cautiously on heavy overcards. If he 3‑bets large I’ll fold this marginal offsuit hand preflop.

In heads-up play with two players remaining (hand 125 of 150), I'm in the big blind with KsJc against an aggressive opponent (Minni). Minni raised to 300 from the button/small blind.

KJ offsuit is a solid hand in heads-up poker where folding equity is much higher and hand ranges are wider. The raise to 300 is standard 3x, and I need to call 200 more.

Key considerations:
1. Stack sizes: I have 15,130 (vulnerable), Minni has 24,470 (larger stack)
2. This is near the end of the tournament (hand 125/150), so building my stack is critical
3. Minni has been consistently aggressive throughout the session
4. KJ is definitely in my continuing range here
5. Reraising applies pressure and can win the pot preflop or help me build a pot when I have equity

Given my shorter stack and the need to be aggressive, I'll reraise to 800 total. This is about 2.67x Minni's bet and shows strength while being sized appropriately for my stack situation. This puts pressure on Minni and either wins the pot or allows me to play postflop with a strong hand.

I opened to 300 on the button with 7♥5♣ and Claude 3‑bet to 800. To call 500 into the current 1100 pot I need ~31.25% equity (500/1600). 7‑5 offsuit is poorly positioned against a 3‑bet range (often dominated by Ax and broadways) and has very limited post‑flop playability; 4‑bet or shoving with this holding is not reasonable. With deep stacks calling would commit me to large pots where I’m usually behind, so the +EV play is to fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

Heads-up position play with QJ. This is a premium enough hand to raise from the button in a 2-player game. Raising to 250 (2.5x the BB) is standard aggression to take the initiative, leverage position, and put pressure on Minni. If she folds, I win the blinds; if she calls, I have position and a decent hand to play post-flop.

Heads-up vs Claude’s open to 250 from the button: I’m in the big blind with 5♣4♣. Calling is 150 into a 350 pot (pot becomes 500), so pot odds ~3.33:1 (≈23% equity needed). 5-4 suited has decent equity and postflop playability heads-up against a wide opening range, and stacks are deep so there’s good implied odds if I hit. I’m OOP postflop, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet; Claude has shown willingness to call/reraised preflop in this game. Plan: call to see a flop and proceed based on texture (play draws/connected boards aggressively, fold or defend cautiously on dry boards facing big aggression).

Flop: 4h 2d 5h; my hand 5c4c = two pair (5s and 4s). Heads‑up this is a very strong holding vs Claude’s open — his range will include many worse pairs, single pairs, overcards and some two‑heart combos; the main real danger is sets (pocket 5s or 4s) but those combos are limited. I’m out of position so I want value and to charge draws while keeping the pot manageable. Bet ~1/2 pot (250 into 500) extracts from worse pairs and gets called by heart draws while denying some equity; if Claude raises I’ll re‑evaluate (small raise = call and proceed cautiously, very large/committed raise = likely fold/reassess).

I'm holding QJ on a 4h-2d-5h flop in a heads-up situation. Minni has raised the flop to 250 after calling my pre-flop raise. In heads-up play, aggression is elevated and flop raises are common. My QJ is Queen-high with decent potential to improve—any Q or J gives me a pair, and I could pick up straight draws on the turn. The pot odds (3:1) are reasonable for a call. Raising here would be too aggressive given the board's dryness and my modest hand strength. I should call to gather more information and see the turn card. If Minni has a marginal hand or air, I might outdraw her or win on a subsequent street. Folding would be too weak in heads-up play with a card like QJ.

I have two pair (5s and 4s) heads‑up on a 4♥‑2♦‑5♥‑7♣ runout. Claude’s calling range to my flop half‑pot lead is wide (worse pairs, single pairs, heart draws, some backdoor/connector hands) and the turn is mostly a blank — it only really helps pocket 7s (few combos) or completes rare straights. I want value and to charge heart flush draws while keeping the pot from getting bloated out of position. Pot is 1,000 so a ~1/2 pot turn bet is the right sizing: big enough to extract from worse pairs/draws and deny free cards, but small enough to keep weaker hands calling and to avoid committing stacks to a likely set. If Claude raises a small/standard-sized raise I’ll call and re‑evaluate; a large, committed raise would credibly represent trips or a set and I’d fold or proceed cautiously. Action: bet 500.

Heads-up call with QJ on a dry board. Minni has shown aggression pre-flop, flop, and turn. My Queen-Jack has two live overcards (8 outs) plus showdown value. Pot odds are 3:1, and I only need ~25% equity to call correctly. Against Minni's likely range (flush draws, small pairs, or semi-bluffs), I have solid equity, especially if she's running a bluff or has a draw. The turn card (7c) doesn't significantly help either of us, so calling to see the river is prudent. This also allows me to gather more information before making a river decision. Folding would be too weak given the pot odds and hand strength in a heads-up situation.

I have two pair (5♣4♣) on a fairly dry board (4♥ 2♦ 5♥ 7♣ J♠). Claude opened/called through the hand (raised pre, called my half‑pot flop and turn bets), so his continuing range contains lots of single pairs (7x, 4x), worse two‑pair combos, some curious calls and occasional bluffs; the main real fear is sets (55/44/22), but those combos are limited. This is a clear value spot heads‑up — my hand beats most of his calling range and the river Jack doesn’t meaningfully improve many of his likely hands. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (1000 into 2000) to extract value from worse pairs and get called by marginal holdings while keeping the pot manageable OOP. If Claude raises substantially, I’ll treat it as a strong line (likely trips/set) and re‑evaluate (small raise I can call and proceed cautiously; a large, committed raise I’d fold).

Calling river with pair of Jacks (QJ on 4h-2d-5h-7c-Js board). Minni's aggressive line could represent various holdings in heads-up play. With 3:1 pot odds, I only need ~25% equity to call profitably. A made pair has decent showdown value against her likely range of bluffs, semi-bluffs, and marginal hands. The risk-reward is favorable given the pot odds and the competitive nature of heads-up play where aggression is elevated.

Heads-up on the button with A♥3♣ — a playable ace with positional advantage. I want to take the initiative, deny Claude a cheap flop and extract value from worse aces and small pairs, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x BB. With both stacks deep this size (300 total) pressures calling ranges but doesn’t bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Postflop plan: c-bet dry/A or 3 flops, pot-control or check-back on dangerous runouts, and fold to big 3-bets or heavy turn/river aggression unless I’ve improved. Overall this is an aggressive, position-first play to maximize EV over the remaining hands.

J9o is a marginal but playable hand in heads-up play with favorable pot odds (3:1). Minni's frequent button raises indicate a wide range. Calling preserves chips while maintaining tournament competitiveness in a 2-player situation. Folding too often from BB would be exploitable.

Checking with J9o on 4h-Th-Ac. While I whiffed top pair, I have strong drawing equity with an open-ended straight draw (8 outs for K or Q) plus two overcards. In heads-up play, checking is a sound line to control pot growth while maintaining information about Minni's hand strength. If Minni bets, I can make an informed decision about calling based on pot odds and my estimated equity. Checking also prevents overplaying a marginal holding and allows me to realize my draw equity cheaply on future streets.

Heads-up on an A♣ T♥ 4♥ flop I have top pair with A♥3♣ — the nut ace but a very weak kicker and two hearts on board. Claude checked, so I should take the initiative: c-bet for value from worse aces, tens and medium pairs and to charge heart/straight draws (deny free cards). A ~2/3-pot bet gives fold equity and protects my hand without bloating the pot given the weak kicker and stacks. If Claude raises big I will strongly respect it (fold or pot-control depending on size); if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate.

With J9 on 4h-Th-Ac in heads-up play, I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to make a straight with any K or Q) plus overcard potential. The pot odds are favorable at 3.5:1 (need ~22% equity to break even), and my hand has approximately 32% equity with two cards to come. Given Minni's aggressive button raising pattern throughout the tournament and my reasonable draw strength, calling is the correct play. Folding too frequently from the big blind in heads-up would be exploitable. I preserve chips while maintaining equity and tournament competitiveness.

On the turn with J9 and a 4h-Th-Ac-Kh board, I hold only J-high with 4 Queen outs to make a broadway straight (~9% equity). Given my short stack disadvantage (12,830 vs 25,770), I should preserve chips and check down this street. My hand has insufficient strength and equity to warrant a bet—betting gains nothing if called by better hands and fails to generate value. Checking allows me to realize my draw equity cheaply on the river while maintaining chip preservation and tournament viability in heads-up play.

I have top pair (A♥) with a very weak 3-kicker on a dangerous board: 4♥ T♥ A♣ K♥. I c-bet the flop and Claude called, so his range contains many hands that now either already beat me (KT, KX/TX pockets, sets, AA, QJ straight, or two hearts for a flush) or have strong turn/river equity. The turn K♥ is a hostile card that both brings more two‑pair/set combos and a third heart, so betting here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and invite check‑raises or big calls from hands that dominate me. With deep stacks and the pot still relatively small I prefer pot control and to realize my equity (I also have the nut‑heart blocker to a nut‑flush). I’ll check and re‑evaluate on the river: if Claude checks back I can consider a small value bet vs worse aces/tens; if he barrels small I will call a reasonable size for pot odds/implied odds, but fold to large river aggression unless I improve to two‑pair/flush/trips.

River with J9 on 4h-Th-Ac-Kh-6d. I hold only jack-high with no made hand. Given my significant stack disadvantage (12,830 vs 25,770 in heads-up), betting here is a pure bluff with no equity backup. Checking is correct because: (1) it preserves my short stack, (2) allows me to see if Minni will bet (enabling an informed fold), and (3) if she checks, I may win a showdown with J-high if she has worse. Betting accomplishes nothing except potentially losing additional chips. Chip preservation is critical at this stack depth in heads-up tournament play.

I have A♥3♣ on A♣ T♥ 4♥ K♥ 6♦ — top pair but a very weak kicker and a dangerous board (K and two hearts). Claude called my flop c-bet (400) then checked turn and river, which looks like a passive/medium-strength line (Txs, Kx, small pairs, some Ax) and less often the nuts (sets, AK, A4). I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal kicker, but I can still extract thin value from worse tens and weaker A‑x hands that will check-call a small bet. I’ll make a small value bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) to get those calls while preserving fold equity and an easy fold-to-raise plan — if Claude raises big I’ll fold and preserve my stack. Bet 500 chips to keep the pot controlled and collect thin value when appropriate.

J-high cannot win without Minni bluffing, which is unlikely given her river aggression after a turn check. Despite acceptable pot odds (3.8:1), the hand has insufficient win probability against her range. With my 12,830-chip stack still providing ~6.8BB, I should preserve chips for better spots rather than call with a fundamentally weak hand. Folding is correct chip preservation strategy in this short-stacked heads-up situation.

Td2h is a weak hand that doesn't justify opening the button heads-up, even in position. With 22 hands remaining and a chip deficit, I should preserve my stack for spots with better equity. Folding allows me to wait for premium or semi-premium hands where I have stronger fold and hand equity balance.

Heads-up on the button with J♥8♥ — a playable, suited, connected hand that benefits hugely from position. Claude in the big blind will defend a wide range, so I want to seize the initiative and use fold equity rather than just limp. A standard 2xBB open (raise to 200 total) is small relative to my 27k stack (<1%) but accomplishes building the pot when I’m ahead, disguising my range, and giving me a lot of postflop maneuvering room. If Claude calls I’ll play the flop in position; if he 3-bets big I can fold or reevaluate based on sizing. Raise to 200 to maximize expected value here.

8-3 offsuit is the worst hand in poker with minimal equity against virtually any raising range. While heads-up play requires some blind defense and the pot odds appear 4:1, the fundamental weakness of this hand makes folding correct. With a healthy stack of 12,680 chips, there's no urgency to play marginal spots.

In heads-up play, button ranges are much wider, but Td2h is too weak even by those standards. The hand lacks both straight and flush potential, has poor kicker quality, and will be extremely difficult to play profitably post-flop. With 20 hands remaining and a chip disadvantage, folding marginal hands is correct strategy - we want to conserve chips for premium hands where we have better equity. The 3-to-1 pot odds don't overcome the fundamental weakness of this holding.

Heads-up on the button with Tc7h is a limp/call or open-raise spot — I prefer aggression in position. A standard 3x BB open (to 300 total) applies pressure, steals the blind often, and gives me initiative postflop. With deep stacks I avoid a tiny 2bb open that gives too-good odds to defend/3-bet; if Claude 3-bets large I'll fold this marginal hand, if he calls I'll c-bet many flops and use position to realize equity. Raise to 300 keeps range wide and exploits button advantage.

I'm in the Big Blind heads-up against Minni with K6o. While K6 is a weak hand in general, in heads-up play this has reasonable playability. Minni has been aggressive from the button throughout the session, likely opening a wide range. The pot is offering me 2:1 odds to call (200 to win 400), which is acceptable given heads-up dynamics. K6 can flop top pair, strong kicker combinations, or develop into strong hands on later streets. With my shorter stack (12,530 to her 27,070), I need to selectively build chips, and calling to see a flop with a hand that has decent equity is better than folding too much. I can reassess post-flop based on board texture and continuation bet patterns I've observed from Minni.

Heads-up against Minni with K6o on a 6h-3s-9d flop. I have a pair of sixes with a King kicker - a decent but marginal hand. Checking allows me to control the pot size and gather information about Minni's holding. Since I'm in position to act after her bet, checking gives me the option to call reasonable bets, fold to larger aggression, or check-raise if she shows weakness. With a shorter stack (12,330 vs 27,070), pot control is important when holding hands that are vulnerable to being dominated. Minni has been aggressively opening from the button, so she could have many hands here - both better (AK, higher pairs) and worse hands (AQ, AT, etc.). Checking is the lower-variance option that keeps the hand in play.

I opened to 300 preflop and have position heads‑up. The flop 6h-3s-9d is dry and misses a big portion of Claude’s calling range; I have no pair but a small gutshot to an 8 and the initiative. I’ll continuation-bet for value/fold equity — keep it about half the pot to deny easy odds and pick up the pot when he has overcards or air. Bet 300; if Claude raises big I’ll fold this unimproved hand and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

Calling on the flop with K6o (pair of sixes with King kicker) on a 6h-3s-9d board. Pot odds are 3:1, and I need only ~25% equity to call profitably. My hand likely has 35-45% equity against Minni's wide aggressive range. With a shorter stack (12,330), I need to build chips selectively with hands that have decent equity and showdown value. Calling keeps the hand in play while maintaining optionality on the turn to assess the runout and Minni's betting patterns. Folding would be overly tight given the pot odds and my stack situation.

Checking down with pair of sixes and King kicker on a 6h-3s-9d-8c board after Minni checks the turn. The 8c is a blank that doesn't help most holdings. With a shorter stack (12K vs 27K), I want to conserve chips while maintaining showdown value. Minni's turn check suggests weakness or pot control, so betting risks getting raised off the hand or inflating the pot with a marginal holding. Checking takes a free card to the river where I can see the final runout and make a more informed decision, while preserving my stack for stronger spots. This is a defensible hand that has decent equity versus her likely range but isn't strong enough to build the pot with a bet.

The turn (8c) completed my hand — with Tc7h on 6h-3s-9d-8c I have a 10-high straight (6-7-8-9-10). Claude called my flop c-bet, so his range contains many one-pair hands (9x, 6x), underpairs, 8x, and some floats. Betting for value is correct: I want to extract from pairs and weaker 8/9 holdings while keeping worse hands in. A half-pot bet (~600 into 1200) is big enough to build the pot and get calls from those hands but small enough to avoid folding out too much showdown value. If Claude check-raises very large I’ll strongly consider calling since very few hands beat my straight (mostly the same T7 straight), but I size to induce calls rather than protect against impossible outs. Bet 600.

Calling with K6o (pair of sixes + King kicker) on 6h-3s-9d-8c board. Getting 3:1 pot odds requiring only 25% equity to break even. My hand likely has 30-40% equity against Minni's aggressive range which includes many holdings (AK, AQ, broadway cards, potential bluffs) that are either dominated by or coin-flip situations against my pair. With a shorter stack in heads-up endgame (19 hands left), I need to accept favorable spots. The 8c turn is a blank. Taking the river to see if my pair holds or if additional equity emerges is the mathematically sound decision.

Checking the river with pair of sixes and King kicker on a 6h-3s-9d-8c-Qd board. Minni checked the turn after betting flop and turn, suggesting a medium-strength hand or missed draw. Betting would rarely get value - worse hands have likely given up and better hands would call. With a short stack (11,430), preserving chips while taking marginal holdings to showdown is correct. My pair has decent showdown value against Minni's likely range of broadway cards and air. Checking allows the hand to resolve while maintaining stack for remaining 19 hands.

I have Tc7h on 6h-3s-9d-8c-Qd — I made the 10-high straight on the turn (6-7-8-9-10). Claude’s line (check-call flop 300, check-call turn 600, check river) looks like one-pair/underpair holdings (9x, 6x, 8x) or stubborn calls — he’s shown he’s willing to call medium-to-large bets. The river Q doesn’t change much and rarely beats my straight, so the objective is to extract value from single-pair hands and any stubborn chips. A ~2/3-pot value bet (1600 into 2400) is large enough to get calls from 9s/8s/top-pair type hands but not so huge it folds them all; if Claude check-raises I’ll be calling most reasonable sizes because I’m effectively at the nuts. Bet 1600.

Calling with K6o (pair of sixes, King kicker) on a 6h-3s-9d-8c-Qd board. Pot odds of 4.1:1 require only 19.5% equity, and my hand likely has 25-35% equity against Minni's river betting range which includes broadway hands and bluffs. In a heads-up endgame with 19 hands remaining and a short stack (11,430), accepting marginal calls with positive equity is necessary to build chips. The King kicker provides showdown value against many hands in her aggressive range.

In heads-up play, I'm on the button/small blind with K6o. While K6 is a marginal hand, the position is premium and I only need to call 50 chips to see a flop when the pot is already 150. In heads-up poker, hand ranges expand significantly from the button, and K6o is within my calling range here. Minni has a significant chip lead (30,070 vs 9,780), so I should be looking to play pots from favorable positions. Calling here allows me to see the flop with position and optionality against a wide big blind range. This is a standard play in heads-up dynamics.

Heads-up, Claude merely limped the small blind (called 50). I’m in the big blind with 9♦8♠ — a playable, connected hand heads-up but I’ll be out of position postflop. I have a big chip advantage (30k vs ~9.7k) and want to exploit his limp/call tendencies by taking the initiative. Raising isolates the pot, gets fold equity, and lets me dictate the action. A 3x BB raise to 300 is big enough to discourage weak speculative calls while keeping the pot reasonable if called. Plan: if he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable flops and control size on dry boards.

Calling K6o from the button in heads-up with 2:1 pot odds. While K6 is marginal, it's within my button calling range against Minni's wide raising distribution. I need position and decent equity to proceed, which I have here. K6o is approximately 40-45% against a wide range, meeting my mathematical threshold. Additionally, I cannot be exploited by folding too tightly or I'll lose by attrition. Post-flop play will determine the trajectory based on the board texture.

I was the preflop aggressor vs Claude’s limp-call with a much bigger stack. Flop Jc–7c–Jh is paired and doesn’t give me any pair or a club draw, but heads‑up his limp/call range is wide and will miss this board a lot. A continuation bet applies pressure, folds out many weak pairs/overcards, and exploits my stack advantage without committing too much. I’ll make a standard 2/3‑pot c‑bet (400). If he calls I’ll reassess on the turn and mostly pot‑control; if he raises I’ll give credit to a very strong hand (trips/full house/quad) and fold.

K6o is a complete whiff on the JJ7 board with no pair, no draw, and limited equity against Minni's flop raising range. Against likely holdings (pocket pairs, strong broadway, semi-bluffs), I'm estimating around 20-25% equity—below the 28.6% needed for proper pot odds. My stack is critically short at 9,530, and calling commits me to difficult decision-making on the turn. With 19 hands remaining, I should preserve chips for stronger holdings where equity is clearer. This is a straightforward fold.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦5♣ — a marginal hand but very playable in heads-up. I already posted the 50 SB, and facing only the big blind I want the initiative and to play post‑flop in position. A standard 3x open (to 300 total) is big enough to fold out weaker hands, discourage loose flats/cheap flops, and still keeps the pot manageable against Claude’s ~9,430 stack. If he 3‑bets I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing; if he calls I’ll use positional advantage to steer post‑flop play. Raising is better than limp/call here.

Folding Q-4o from the big blind. This is a marginal hand at best - queen with a 4-kicker offers poor equity against Minni's raising range from the button. Playing out of position with weak holdings is a leak in profitable poker. With 18 hands remaining and a moderate stack, I should preserve chips for premium spots with better cards and positioning. The pot odds (2:1) don't justify defending with such a weak hand against an aggressive opponent.

In heads-up play with a weak hand like 2-5o, calling to see a flop from the button is reasonable. Already invested 50 in the small blind, getting 3:1 odds to call another 50. Position postflop is valuable. Short-stacked, so need to preserve chips and avoid marginal raising situations. Minni is aggressive and won't fold easily to min-raises. Call to control pot size and evaluate the flop.

5♣7♦ is a weak, marginal hand heads‑up and Claude simply limped the SB — likely a wide, often passive range. There is no cost to check (I already have the BB posted) and raising to 200 will mostly get called and put me OOP in a multi‑street pot with very little equity. I’ll check behind to see a flop and only invest further if I pick up a meaningful piece (pair/two‑pair/draw) or get a very profitable read/spot; otherwise I can fold to heavy aggression. Checking preserves stack and keeps options open against a shorter, unpredictable opponent.

I have 5♣7♦ on Jd Jc 8d — no pair and essentially no realistic draw. Claude limp-called preflop so his range is wide but contains many hands that beat me (any J, any 8, most pocket pairs and overcards with showdown value). The pot is tiny (200) relative to both stacks, and betting OOP as a donk with no fold equity or blockers is unnecessary risk. I’ll check and keep the pot small — fold to meaningful aggression and only invest if I pick up a real piece or see a clear bluff opportunity on a later street.

With 2-5 offsuit on a J-J-8 board, I have the weakest possible hand with zero equity and no draws. Minni checked, indicating weakness but this doesn't justify betting trash. Short-stacked at 9330 chips, I need to preserve resources. Checking controls pot size, keeps options open on the turn, and allows me to evaluate Minni's response with a clearer picture. This is a standard check-fold scenario if Minni bets.

The turn gives me a real piece of the board — I’ve made a pair of sevens (5♣7♦ with J♦ J♣ 8♦ 7♥). Claude limp‑called preflop and checked the flop, so his range is wide and contains a lot of hands I now beat (overcards, weak pairs, some small pairs), though any Jack, an 8 or a set still crush me. The pot is tiny (200) relative to stacks, so I want a small value bet to extract from worse hands and deny free cards while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll fire a modest bet to get called by worse and to fold out pure air; if Claude raises strongly I’ll assume a Jack/8/set and fold/re‑evaluate. Bet 300.

With 2-5 offsuit on a J-J-8-7 board, I have zero equity and cannot win unless bluffing, which is not applicable as the caller. Minni's turn raise after a flop check indicates either a made hand or a value-based aggression. Short-stacked at 9330 chips, calling 300 just commits more chips to a pot I cannot win. No draws, no pair, no path to victory. Fold to preserve remaining chips for playable situations.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening a wide range. J♠3♦ is weak but playable in heads-up and I want to apply pressure to Claude’s big blind rather than passively complete. A raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) gives meaningful fold equity while keeping the bet size reasonable with our deep stack; if he flats I keep range advantage and act last on later streets, if he 3-bets big I’ll fold this marginal hand. I’ll take the initiative and raise to 300 to try to steal the pot or play postflop in position.

Premium hand (pocket kings) in heads-up play. Should raise to build pot and apply pressure. Minni's button raise range is wide enough that kings are likely ahead. A 2.67x raise of the initial bet (800 vs 300) is standard aggression that builds the pot without overcommitting given stack sizes. This hand plays well postflop in position even if facing a reraise, I can evaluate then.

J♠3♦ is a very weak offsuit hand with poor equity and terrible playability against a 3‑bet. The current pot is 1,100 and it costs me 500 more to call (I’d need ~31.25% equity to break even). Versus Claude’s 3‑bet-to‑800 range my hand is usually dominated and rarely has the fold equity or post‑flop play to justify investing more chips. I’m also the chip leader (30,370 vs his 8,530) in a two‑hand match — no need to gamble marginally and risk a big chunk of my stack with junk. Fold and wait for a much better spot to apply pressure or shove.

Raising with AK on the button in heads-up play is standard aggression. With a short stack (9,580) and premium hand, I need to apply pressure and pick up chips. The raise to 200 (minimum raise) is efficient—it applies pressure while preserving chips if called. In heads-up play with limited hands remaining (15), AK justifies aggressive play from position.

Heads-up in the big blind with Ac5o facing a small open to 200. I’ve already posted 100, so calling 100 more (200 total committed) is a small price to see a flop against what is likely a very wide SB opening range. A5o has decent equity and an Ace blocker reduces some of Claude’s strong-A combos; the pot odds (~100 into a 300 pot) justify a call. I’m out of position post-flop, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet — I'll call, see the flop, and proceed based on texture (c-bet for value on favorable flops, check/fold or control the pot on dry boards).

Heads-up vs a wide button open, I’ll take the initiative and c-bet ~1/2 pot. I have Ac5s: Ace blocker (reduces opponent’s strong A‑combos), plus backdoor outs (≈16% to hit a 4 for the wheel by river, ≈12.5% to pair the A by river, very small backdoor flush ≈4%). The 2-3-8 with two spades is a board that misses a lot of a typical open-raising range, so a half-pot bet charges spade draws, gets value/folds from worse K/Q/J‑high hands, and keeps the pot manageable out of position. Plan: bet 200; fold to a large raise (he’s representing a strong made hand), call small-to-medium calls and re-evaluate on the turn (barrel with A/4 or continue for value/protection on favorable cards, check/control otherwise).

Calling with AK on 2-3-8 flop in heads-up play. I have 8 clean outs to top pair and sufficient pot odds (3:1) with good equity against Minni's likely range. Short stack situation (15 hands left) calls for playing decent hands like AK. A 200 chip call is minimal relative to my 9,430 stack. The board texture is favorable and I have two cards to improve.

Heads-up on the turn with Ac5s; I c-bet the flop (2d 3s 8s) and got called. The 7c is a relatively harmless blank — it doesn’t complete the spade draws and only helps a narrow subset of Claude’s range (hands with a 7). His calling range to the flop likely contains pairs (8x, 3x, 2x), single-pair hands, and spade draws. Betting again applies pressure: it charges his draws, folds out many overcards and weak holdings, and extracts value from worse pairs (8s, 3s, 2s) that called the flop. Pot math: pot is 800 — a 400 bet (~1/2 pot) makes him face 400 to call into 1200 (≈25% equity required), which is a stiff price for many floats/draws. I’ll continue my aggression with a half‑pot barrel to both get value and deny equity; if he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a large shove/raise given I only have Ace‑high), but I’m happy to take the pot now or realize my ~15%–20% equity to improve on the river.

Calling with AK on the turn in heads-up play is mathematically sound. The 4:1 pot odds significantly exceed my ~20% break-even requirement with ~40% equity against Minni's likely range. With only 15 hands remaining and a short stack (9,230), folding premium hands like AK would be excessively nitty. The board texture (2-3-8-7) is relatively dry but has many cards that complete my hand (any A, K, or Q). One card to improve and chase river value.

Check. The river 5h gives me a single pair (pair of 5s). I was the aggressor on flop and turn and Claude called both streets, so his continuing range is weighted toward made hands (8x, 7x, 3x, other pocket pairs) and some spade draws — many of which still beat a lone pair of fives. Betting for thin value is unattractive: there are few worse hands that will continue (maybe 3x/2x or a lone 5), and a river bet will often get called or raised by better hands. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize showdown value, and keeps the option to call small bluffs if he bets. Plan: check and call a small-to-medium value bluff, but fold to a large shove representing a very strong holding.

Checking on the river with AK high against Minni's check. She was aggressive through the turn (raising to 400) but checked the river, signaling weakness. My ace-high has decent showdown value. Given my short stack (8,830) and only 15 hands remaining in the tournament, preserving chips while seeing a showdown is the correct strategic decision. Betting would risk chips against an opponent showing weakness when I have a reasonable hand to show down.

I have 2♦9♥ — a very weak offsuit hand. This is heads-up with me on the button (I act first preflop but last postflop) and I’ve already posted the 50 small blind. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 more to see a flop (complete to 100 total). Calling is the lowest-variance, most +EV option here: I get good pot odds to see a flop and retain positional advantage postflop. Raising with junk like 2-9o bloats the pot out of line and creates difficult postflop spots; folding is too tight given the cheap price to see a flop heads-up. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to substantial preflop 3-bets, and on the flop proceed passively with misses (check/fold vs big pressure) or pursue small bets / pot control if I hit a pair or a usable draw.

Check with 4h Ts from the BB. Minni's passive small blind call suggests weakness. With a marginal hand, I should see the flop at no additional cost. If I hit something valuable on the flop, I can proceed; if I miss, I can fold without additional investment. This is a prudent approach in late-stage tournament play with limited chip equity.

Checking with 4h Ts on 7s-Qs-Qh. This hand is complete air with minimal straight draw potential. Minni has also checked, indicating her weakness. With a paired, high-card board and limited stack resources late in the tournament, checking through to see the turn card is the correct approach. If the turn provides a straight draw (6, 8, 9, or J), I can reassess, but otherwise this is likely a fold-to-bet situation.

I have 2♦9♥ and completely missed the flop (Qs Qh 7s). Claude checked, and on a paired board with two spades I have no pair or relevant draw and no meaningful blockers. Betting here would bloat the pot with pure air and is unlikely to fold enough better hands (or it will get called/raised by queens or strong holdings). I’ll check to control the pot, take a free turn card if he checks back, and fold to any meaningful bet (preserve my big stack and avoid unnecessary bluffs into a likely made hand).

With 4h-Ts on a 7s-Qs-Qh-4c board, I've hit a modest pair of fours. Minni's passive checking on the flop suggests she doesn't have a strong hand, but I'm still vulnerable to any Queen or seven in her range. In a short-stack situation late in the tournament (Hand 137/150 with 8.7k chips), checking to see the river for free is prudent. This preserves chips while allowing me to reach showdown if the river doesn't improve the board further. If Minni has air, I can win the pot; if she has a better hand, I minimize losses.

I have 2♦9♥ and have missed the board (Qs Qh 7s 4c). Claude checked flop and checked the turn — I have no pair, no spade draw and virtually no showdown value. Betting here would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity against a passive/check‑call opponent and would needlessly bloat the pot given my massively deeper stack and many hands left in the tournament. I’ll control the pot and preserve chips: check the turn and plan to fold to any meaningful bet. If Claude checks back to the river I can reassess, but for now checking is the lowest variance, +EV line.

I'm on the river with 4h-Ts on a board of 7s-Qs-Qh-4c-5d, giving me a pair of fours. This is a weak hand on a scary, paired board with a Queen pair community cards. Throughout this hand, both Minni and I have checked the flop and turn, indicating mutual weakness or reluctance to commit. With 8730 chips remaining and 14 hands left to play (hand 137/150), I need to be selective about where I commit my chips. My pair of fours will beat most air hands if we go to showdown, but I'm vulnerable to any Queen, Seven, or Four in Minni's holdings. Checking preserves my stack while keeping the pot small with a marginal holding. If Minni bets the river, I can make a fold decision based on bet sizing and my risk/reward at this stage. The passive play pattern suggests a showdown is likely the best outcome here rather than engaging in a river bet-call dynamic.

I have 2♦9♥ and completely missed this board (Qs Qh 7s 4c 5d). Both players checked flop and turn and Claude checked the river — I have no pair, no draw and virtually zero showdown value. Betting here would be a pure bluff with very limited fold equity (Claude can have Qx, 77, small pairs, or simply call with any marginal hand) and would needlessly bloat the pot when I have a large stack and many hands left in the tournament. I'm in position and can pick better spots to apply pressure later. Optimal low-variance, +EV line is to check and fold to any river bet. Action: check.

Q7o is a standard raising hand from the button in heads-up play. It has reasonable equity against Minni's likely big blind range and allows me to take the initiative and leverage positional advantage. A raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is standard sizing that applies appropriate pressure without overcommitting with a marginal holding.

Heads‑up on the button Claude opened to 250 (≈2.5BB). I’m in the big blind with 5♠8♦ — a marginal but defendable hand heads‑up. To call I must put in 150 more (making my total in the pot 250); that gives me 150/(350+150) ≈ 30% break‑even equity. Versus a wide HU opening range my hand has roughly that much equity, so a defend call is +EV. I won’t 3‑bet here: 5‑8o OOP plays poorly if called and a min‑3bet to 400 buys little fold equity while creating difficult post‑flop decisions out of position. With my very deep stack I can apply pressure later — keep the pot small preflop, see a flop, and proceed based on texture (raise or lead if I hit a pair/draw; fold or check‑control if I miss and face a big c‑bet). Call 150 to see the flop and keep options open.

I missed this K♠‑9♥‑4♦ rainbow flop with 5♠8♦ — no pair and only very weak backdoor outs. Heads‑up Claude opened preflop with a wide raising range but it contains many Kx combos, so checking lets him c‑bet and forces me to call with <33% required equity vs a typical 1/2‑pot c‑bet. Calling out of position is -EV. I’d rather take the initiative and apply fold equity: bet ~1/2 pot (250) representing a King/strong hand to steal the pot now. If he folds I win; if he calls I can proceed cautiously on the turn; if he raises I’ll fold given my lack of showdown value. Betting 250 is the high‑EV play here.

Q7o is too weak to commit chips against Minni's flop raise on a 9h-4d-Ks board. In heads-up play, preserving chips for premium holdings is more valuable than playing marginal hands out of position. Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with Q♦8♣. I’ve already posted 50 and can just complete to 100, but in heads-up position Q8o is in my open-raise range. Claude (BB, 8580) has shown a fairly loose/aggressive tendency, so taking the initiative from the button is profitable. I’ll raise to 300 (standard 3x-ish sizing) to: (1) steal the blind more often, (2) define the hand and isolate Claude, and (3) use positional advantage postflop. 3s Jc is a weak hand from the big blind out of position. While I'm short-stacked at 8580 chips versus Minni's 31020, folding Jack-three offsuit is correct. Minni has been active from the button, and this hand doesn't have the equity to warrant a defensive play. With 11 hands remaining, I should preserve chips for stronger spots where I can better contest pots. A fold here is disciplined and keeps my remaining stack intact for more favorable situations.

Folding J3o from the button in heads-up play. While I'm short-stacked at 85 BBs, this is a weak, gapped hand even by heads-up standards. With only 10 hands remaining in the tournament, I should be selective and wait for premium hands (pairs, broadway combinations, AK/AQ) to make aggressive moves. Preserving chips for spots with better equity is more valuable than marginal situations with J3o.

Heads-up on the button with pocket fours — too good to just fold and worth taking the initiative. I want to isolate the big blind and generate fold equity while keeping a standard sizing. Raising to 300 (≈3x the BB) is a normal heads-up open: it pressures Claude, narrows his defending range, and lets me play the flop in position. If Claude 3-bets big I can fold this small pair; if he calls I can use position to continuation-bet or pot-control based on the flop texture.

Calling with K8o in heads-up BB. Checking K8o on an Axx flop in heads-up. My hand has minimal equity against Minni's wide range and no meaningful draws. I’ve flopped a set: I hold 4♠4♥ and the board is 4♣ A♠ 2♥ — very strong. Claude checked from the big blind, which keeps in a wide range: Ax hands, broadways, weak pairs, and some pocket pairs. The only realistic hand that currently beats me is pocket A-A (set of aces), which is possible but not super common given he just cold-called my button open. I want value from Ax hands, medium pairs and any stray pocket pairs, and I also want to deny a free card that could bring scary overcards. A half‑pot bet (~50% pot) balances extraction and pot control: it keeps worse hands and A‑high hands in, but doesn’t overcommit me if Claude check‑raises (which would polarize his range toward very strong hands). Pot=600, so I’ll bet 300. Plan: if called I’ll continue to extract on later streets while re-evaluating if the turn brings an Ace or other scary card; if check‑raised big I’ll respect a very strong range (AA) and proceed cautiously but will often still call a reasonable raise with a set.

Calling K8o on an Axx flop in heads-up play. At 4:1 pot odds, I only need 20% equity to call profitably, and K8o has approximately 30-35% equity against Minni's wide SB opening range. With only 9 hands remaining in the tournament and a stack of 8,230 chips, I cannot afford to fold too frequently in marginal spots. The pot odds are favorable enough to justify seeing the turn card, and I can make decisions based on what develops there. Folding would be overly nitty in this late-stage position.

Checking K8o on an Axx board with marginal equity. I have approximately 10 outs (wheel + overcards) giving me ~20-25% equity. Checking allows me to stay in the hand affordably while keeping my options open on the river. If Minni bets, I can make a river decision based on final pot odds and whether I hit my outs.

I have a set of 4s (4♠4♥) on 4♣ A♠ 2♥ 6♥. Claude checked the turn after calling my flop bet, so his range is wide (Ax, broadways, small-to-medium pairs) and only AA realistically beats me. I’ll make a value bet of about 2/3 pot (≈800 into 1200): big enough to get calls from worse hands but small enough to call a reasonable check-raise and re-evaluate if he jams (which would often indicate AA). If he calls I’ll continue extracting on the river when appropriate; if he check‑raises big I’ll respect a very strong range and re-evaluate.

Calling K8o on the turn with 2.5:1 pot odds. My hand has approximately 25-30% equity against Minni's wide SB range, which is sufficient for a +EV call at these odds. With only 9 hands remaining in the tournament and heads-up play, I cannot afford to fold marginal+EV hands. The K-high with gutter straight draw has enough showdown value and improving outs to justify the call. Folding would be overly tight in this late-stage tournament situation.

Checking K-high on the river with a paired board and ace. My hand has zero showdown value (just K-high on a board with A66), making any bet fundamentally counterproductive. Checking allows me to either win the pot if Minni checks back, or fold if she bets. With only 9 hands remaining in the tournament and a short stack (7,130 chips), I cannot afford inefficient chip loss. Betting would fold out all worse hands while only getting called by hands that beat me. This is the mathematically optimal line on the river when holding the worst possible hand.

The river (4♣ A♠ 2♥ 6♥ 6♦) gives me a full house: 4♠4♥ with the board pair of sixes = 44466 (fours full of sixes). Claude checked after calling my flop and turn bets — his line is consistent with a wide calling range (A‑x, medium pocket pairs, some broadways). The hands that beat me are limited (AA, any hand that now makes a higher full house/quads with a 6), and his passive river check makes those holdings less likely. I want to extract value from the many A‑hands and pocket pairs that will call a value bet, so I’ll bet for value rather than check and give a free showdown. I choose ~2,000 (~70% of the 2,800 pot): big enough to get value from Ax and medium pairs, but not so large that I fold out thin callers or induce only all‑in raises. If Claude jams or check‑raises, his range is polarized and I’ll strongly consider calling — my full house is close to the nuts, and only very rare quads/higher full houses beat me.

K8o on 4c As 2h 6h 6d is K-high with zero equity. My actual equity (~10-12%) falls well short of the 23% needed for profitable 3.4:1 pot odds. Minni's three-street betting line (flop 300, turn 800, river 2000) indicates strong hands like Ax or 66 (now full house). While tournament pressure with 9 hands left is real, calling -EV hands accelerates my exit. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities ahead and is mathematically mandatory here.

Raising to 250 total with A7 from the button in heads-up play. This is a standard 2.5x raise that applies pressure while I'm in position. With a short stack and limited hands remaining (8 hands left), I need to play profitable hands aggressively. A7 is definitely in my raising range from the button against an opponent I've been playing against. The raise takes initiative and allows me to win the pot immediately or play post-flop with a good holding.

Heads-up vs a 250 open from the SB and I'm in the BB with K♥9♠. I’ll call to see a flop and then apply pressure if I hit a King or a good texture; folding is too passive and a 3-bet would bloat the pot OOP unnecessarily.

Out of position on a dry but paired flop (T♥ 3c T♦) with K♥9♠ — I have only one overcard and very little showdown value. The SB’s opening range in heads-up contains lots of hands that connect with this texture (single Tens, small pairs, some sets), so c-betting OOP with a marginal hand is risky and will just bloat the pot. Plan: call small continuation bets to realize equity and try to hit a King/9 on the turn; fold to a large bet or a check-raise; if he checks back I can take a free card or take a stab on a safe turn.

Checking with As7s on Th-3c-Td. My hand is marginal ace-high on a paired board with significant chip disadvantage. Minni's check suggests weakness. Heads-up on a paired-ten board (T♥ 3c T♦ 5c). This is a good spot to apply pressure and try to take the pot down now with a small-to-medium-sized bluff rather than see a cheaper river. I’ll bet a size large enough to fold out many missed hands but small enough not to overcommit (300 into a 500 pot). If Claude raises or shoves I’ll respect the strength and fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river based on the card and his action.

Calling 300 with A7 on a Th-3c-Td-5c board. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds and need only 25% equity to break even. Ace-high has reasonable equity against Minni's likely range here. With 8 hands remaining and a short stack, I should play my marginal hands with positive pot odds rather than folding. A call allows me to reach the river and potentially improve with an ace, while preserving chips versus raising all-in with a borderline hand.

I barreled the turn (300) as a bluff attempt, but Claude called — that call shows real showdown value (likely a Ten, a small/medium pocket pair or a 3) rather than a pure fold-to-pressure hand. The river 4s is a blank that doesn't improve my K9; another barrel requires meaningful fold equity to be profitable (e.g. a 600 bet into the 1,100 pot needs ~35% folds, a 300 bet needs ~21%). With essentially no value and little chance to get him off better hands, I’ll check and avoid throwing more chips away — if he bets I can fold; if he checks we go to showdown and conserve my stack for better spots.

Minni checked on the river, indicating weakness. With A7 on a paired board, I have a marginal but potentially winning hand at showdown. With only 8 hands left and a significant chip disadvantage, I should preserve my stack by checking back and taking the free showdown rather than risking additional chips on a marginal hand that may not be best. This disciplined short-stack play maximizes my equity without overcommitting.

Heads-up on the button (also SB) with K♦2♦ — K2 suited is a clear open in heads-up play. If Claude calls I'll play in position; if he 3-bets I'll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies. Raise to 300 total.

K7 offsuit is a marginal hand, and I'm out of position in the Big Blind facing Minni's raise from the Small Blind. While Minni has been playing aggressively late in the tournament, K7o doesn't justify calling 200 more chips here. The cost-to-call (200 into 400) offers 2:1 odds, but K7o struggles against Minni's wide raising range from the SB, especially out of position post-flop. In heads-up poker from the button, position is paramount and wider ranges are standard. While 4-5 offsuit is normally weak, the heads-up button is one of the few places to play it. A small raise applies pressure, generates fold equity, and takes down the blind immediately if Minni folds. This is consistent with optimal heads-up strategy where button raises wider ranges. My 75BB stack is deep enough to play strategically rather than being forced all-in.

Heads-up in the big blind with K♠? (KhQc). Claude (button/SB) opened to 200. I’ve already posted 100, so calling 100 more (total 200) is a clear, +EV play here. KQo is a very strong heads-up hand vs a wide SB opening range and calling gives me good pot odds (100 to win ~400) to see a flop. I’ll call and evaluate the flop — c-bet/raise on favorable K/Q/A textures, and proceed cautiously on coordinated or ace-high boards where he shows strength.

Heads-up on a dry, low flop (7♥ 3♦ 2♦). Claude opened from the button with a wide range, so he has a lot of missed hands here as well as some diamond draws and small pairs. I hold K♥Q♣ — two overcards with decent equity vs his missed combos but no pair and no diamond. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re-evaluate on the turn (fold to a significant raise unless I improve or the runout is favorable; consider barreling only on safe cards).

4h5c is essentially garbage on a 7h 3d 2d board. I'm drawing thin with only ~15% equity against Minni's betting range. With 6 outs to a weak pair and 4 outs to a 6-high straight, there's no profitable call here. Minni is showing strength from out of position, and I need to be disciplined about my equity requirements in heads-up play. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with J♥9♠ — a playable, positionally-advantaged hand. Against a single BB and with a large chip lead I want the initiative and fold equity, so I’ll open-raise rather than just completing. Min-raising to 3-bet against Minni's min-raise from button. J7o is reasonable for 3-betting in heads-up play, especially with positional advantage post-flop. The small raise size suggests potential weakness, making this a good spot to apply pressure. If called, I have ~12.7:1 odds on remaining stack and position advantage post-flop. This aggressive play is important given short stack status near end of tournament.

Claude 3-bet to 600 after my 300 open. Heads‑up J♥9♠ on the button is a playable hand and plays very well in position. The 3‑bet sizing is relatively small, so his range is likely mixed (value + light 3‑bets) and I get correct pot odds to continue: I must call 300 into a 900 pot (≈25% break‑even). Call to 600 total.

Checking with J7o on a 4-4-A board. This hand has minimal equity against Minni's likely holdings (Ax, pocket pairs from her min-raise/call line). With 6,180 chips remaining and short stack pressure, burning chips on marginal bluffs or weak hands is counterproductive. Checking allows me to fold to a bet and preserve chips for better opportunities in the final 6 hands. If Minni checks back, I can reassess on turn cards. This is a disciplined play prioritizing survival and chip preservation over speculative aggression.

Flop is 4c 4s As; I hold Jh9s. Claude 3‑bet preflop so his range is polarized (value hands + some light 3‑bets), and his check on this fairly dry, paired board can be both weakness and a trap. I’m in position with a huge stack advantage and want to use fold equity rather than passively give him a free card. If he shows strong aggression (a sizable check‑raise) I’ll re‑evaluate and likely release J9. Betting 400 is the right exploitative play.

J7o has minimal equity on a 4-4-A flop, especially against Minni's betting action. After checking the flop, her bet of 400 represents meaningful aggression on a board where my hand has no real strength - I don't have an ace, and jack-high is vulnerable to her likely holdings (Ax, pairs, or broadway combos from her min-raise/call line). With only 6 hands remaining and a short stack of 6780, I need to preserve chips for premium spots. Folding the 400 to call costs me only 400 chips and keeps me alive with 6380 remaining for the final 5 hands. Given my hand weakness and tournament position, this is disciplined chip preservation rather than investing in a marginal spot.

JcQc in heads-up button play is a strong hand that warrants aggression. From the button/small blind, I should be raising with solid holdings to build pots and leverage position. This is a standard 3x raise that allows me to win small pots or get to a favorable flop. With a short stack (6,730), I want to mix aggression with my best hands to maintain chip equity pressure on Minni.

Claude opened to 300 from the button (SB). Pot is 400 and I must call 200. I hold Q♠8♠ — a playable, suited hand in heads‑up with decent equity (good for flush/backdoor possibilities and decent high‑card value). Calling 200 into the 400 pot gives me 200 to win ~600 (≈33% break‑even equity). Versus a wide button opening range heads‑up Q8s has enough equity and post‑flop playability to justify a call. Raising is an option, but Q8s is marginal as a pure 3‑bet (it’s often dominated and would bloat the pot against an opponent with ~65bb effective stack); plus position won’t change postflop (I’ll be out of position on the flop), so I prefer to keep the pot manageable and realize equity. Plan: call to see the flop — if I hit a pair or strong spade draw I’ll apply pressure; on a dry, unfavorable flop I’ll proceed cautiously and fold to heavy aggression.

Flop is Qc Ad Th and I hold Qs8s. I’ve made second pair with a weak 8-kicker and there are no spades on board (so no realistic flush by the river with only two cards left). Claude’s preflop raising range out of the button contains a lot of Ax and Broadway hands that dominate me, and there are also some made straights (KJ) or tens/aces that beat me. I’m first to act on the flop — betting would bloat the pot and force difficult decisions versus a shorter, aggressive opponent when my hand is only marginal. With a large chip advantage I don’t need to gamble here; I prefer pot control and to gather information. Plan: check and evaluate Claude’s reaction. Call small bets on later streets where appropriate, fold to large raises unless I improve, and extract value only when the board runs safe for my pair.

I'm holding Jc9c on a Qc-Ad-Th board—I have significant equity with an open-ended straight draw (8 or K completes K-Q-J-10-9 or Q-J-10-9-8) plus a nine-card flush draw. This is a classic semi-bluff situation. Given my short stack (6,480), I need to play aggressively with strong draws to accumulate chips. Betting ~400 applies pressure on Minni, potentially winning the pot outright while maintaining fold equity. If called, I have roughly 11 outs and can spike a strong made hand on the turn or river. Checking allows Minni to take control with a bet, so the aggressive play maintains my chip pressure and leverage.

Board is Qc Ad Th; I hold Qs8s — second pair with a weak kicker. I checked and Claude fired a modest 400 into the 1000 pot. There are lots of hands in his button raising range (Ax, broadways, tens) that beat me, but he’s also likely to have many weaker Qx/Kx/Jx candidates and outright bluffs. Raising out of position with a vulnerable pair is thin — I’d be bloating the pot and playing a difficult spot against many hands that dominate me. Folding is too weak given his small bet and my showdown value. Calling 400 (≈29% break‑even) keeps the pot controlled, lets me realize equity, and keeps worse hands and bluffs in the pot. Plan: call now, re-evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy pressure if an Ace/Ten lands and he shows strength, but extract/raise only if I improve to two pair/trips or if he gives clear weakness.

I just improved to trips (Qs on Qc–Qh–Ad–Th board) — a very strong value hand heads‑up. Claude was the preflop raiser and c‑bet on the flop, then showed willingness to put a modest 400 on the turn; his range contains a lot of Ax, some Tx, Kx/Broadway hands and occasional bluffs. Very few of those hands beat trips and many of them will call a sizeable raise (A‑x, T‑x, KJ type hands). With a huge stack advantage I want to extract value and deny him a free river card rather than check/call and let him realize equity. I’ll raise to a size that gets called by single‑pair Aces/Tens but isn’t so huge that it only gets action from the absolute nuts. Raise to 2,600 chips total — big enough to charge Ax/Tx and build the pot, but small relative to my stack so I can comfortably call any shove. Plan: if he calls, continue extracting on the river; if he jams, call — trips should be a clear favorite against his likely range.

With JcQc on a Qc-Ad-Th-Qh board facing a 2,600 raise, I need to assess my equity and pot odds carefully. I have an open-ended straight draw (K or 8 gives me a made straight = 8 outs) plus three pair outs (J or 9 = 6 outs), totaling approximately 14 outs, which translates to roughly 30% equity on the river. However, the pot odds require approximately 39% equity to break even (pot of 4,000 to call 2,600). I'm getting insufficient pot odds, and this is hand 146 of 150 with a short stack of 6,080. While aggression is important with few hands remaining, calling here with negative expected value would erode my chip stack unnecessarily. Folding preserves my 6,080 chips for the final four hands where I can find better spot with superior equity. In a short-stacked heads-up situation, chip preservation on marginal spots is crucial.

Heads-up on the button (I posted 50, BB is 100). Jc6h is marginal but playable in position and I should be opening wide here to apply pressure and take the initiative. Claude has ~59BB, so I don't want to shove unnecessarily — a standard 3x BB open is a good balance of fold equity and postflop playability. Raising to 300 builds the pot, denies him a cheap flop, and leaves plenty of stack behind to play postflop in position.

7-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. While I'm in the big blind and getting 2:1 pot odds, hand strength fundamentally matters more than pot odds for unplayable hands. With only 3 hands remaining and a reasonable stack of 5,980 chips, I should fold and preserve my chips for premium opportunities in the final 2 hands. Even against Minni's light raise, 7-2 has virtually no playability postflop and would face an uphill battle.

A6o is a premium hand in heads-up play (top 40% of hands). With only 3 hands left in the tournament and a desperate 5930 vs 33920 chip disadvantage, I cannot afford to play passively with strong holdings. A raise to 400 applies pressure, creates fold equity, and commits a reasonable portion of my stack to build a pot where my hand has solid equity against Minni's big blind range. The Ace gives significant value, and heads-up dynamics favor aggressive play in this spot.

Facing a 400 open from the button (Claude) while I'm in the big blind with 8♣6♦. To call I need 300 more into a 500 pot (pot odds ≈ 1.67:1 → ~37.5% equity). Heads‑up button opens are wide, so 8‑6o has reasonable equity to continue. I’m the big stack (33,920 vs his 5,580) so there’s no need to get aggressive and bloat the pot out of position by 3‑betting; a raise would also commit more chips OOP without a strong hand. Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously out of position — fold to heavy pressure if I completely miss, continue/chase if I pair or pick up a good draw, and extract value when I improve. Therefore I will call the raise and see the flop.

Check. I’m out of position with 8♣6♦ on A♣T♣5♠ — no pair and only a runner-runner club flush possibility (very slim). I have ~6 clean outs to make a pair by the river (modest two‑street equity) and Claude’s HU open will include lots of Ax/Tx that dominate me. Betting OOP with a marginal hand just bloats the pot and plays right into his range. I’ll control the pot and see how he reacts on the flop; fold to a sizable C‑bet, but continue/reevaluate if I pick up a pair or a reasonable draw on the turn.

I'm betting 2000 on top pair (pair of Aces with 6 kicker) after Minni's check on a favorable flop. Minni's passivity suggests a weak hand, and I need to apply maximum pressure in this desperate situation with only 3 hands remaining. This bet size (~2.5x pot) is aggressive but not overcommitting, allowing me to maintain some chips if called. Top pair is very strong here and I should take down this pot or get value from any marginal hands Minni continues with.

Claude has bet 2000 into a 2800 pot. To call I need ~2000/(2800+2000) = 41.7% equity. With 8♣6♦ on A♣T♣5♠ I have only about ~24% to make a pair by the river plus a tiny (~4%) runner‑runner club flush possibility — combined equity well under the break‑even threshold. Claude opened preflop and now fires a large flop sizing with a short stack; his range will contain many Ax/Tx and club‑heavy hands that dominate me. I’m the big stack and don’t need to gamble OOP with a marginal holding or risk getting committed to a losing spot. A shove would be high variance and likely called by many hands that crush me. Folding and preserving my stack to pick better spots is the correct, lower‑variance +EV play here.

Heads-up on the button (small blind). Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 to call, but opening a wide raise from the button is standard to apply pressure to a shorter-stacked big blind. Ten-deuce offsuit is marginal, but raising to the min (200 total) is a low-risk steal attempt that wins the pot often and keeps position postflop if called. Plan: steal the blinds if Claude folds; if he calls use positional advantage to c-bet or control the pot; if he 3-bets/shoves for his ~6.3k I’ll fold T2o to preserve my big stack edge.

Calling 100 more chips to reach a total of 150 (50 initial big blind + 100 call). Q-4 suited is a marginal hand but at 3:1 pot odds, it's worth taking this shot. Short-stacked with limited hands remaining, I need to accumulate chips in spots with reasonable equity and pot odds. The hand has flush draw and queen-high potential post-flop.

Short-stacked with Q♠4♠ on 8♦6♥J♣. With only 2 hands left and 6,180 chips (~30BB), I need to carefully manage spot selection. I have a strong flush draw (9 outs) plus queen-high, which gives reasonable equity. Rather than risk getting into a pot-committing situation by betting, I'll check to control pot size and see opponent action. If she checks back, I get a free card to potentially improve. If she bets, I can make a calculated decision from there. This approach maximizes flexibility in my final 2 hands.

I was the preflop aggressor and this is a dry, two-overcard/rainbow board (J‑8‑6). My T‑2 has no showdown value so this is a pure continuation‑bet bluff to steal the pot and punish Claude’s call‑wide preflop range. Pot is 400 so a half‑pot bet of 200 puts meaningful pressure while keeping the size manageable; if Claude folds I take it down, if he calls I can reevaluate on the turn, and if he raises big I’ll fold and preserve my large stack.

Short-stacked with Q♠4♠ facing a 200 chip bet on 8♦6♥J♣ with only 2 hands remaining. I have a strong flush draw (9 outs) plus three overcards (3 queens), yielding approximately 27-30% equity. The 3:1 pot odds (600:200) require only 25% equity to be profitable. In heads-up tournament poker with 2 hands left and an 30BB stack, this is an acceptable risk. Calling preserves my ability to accumulate chips and gives me outs to improve on the turn and river.

Short-stacked with Q♠4♠ on 8♦6♥J♣7♥. Holding queen-high with spade flush draw (7 outs). Opponent checked, indicating weakness or pot control. With one hand remaining after this, checking preserves chip value while allowing a free river card. If a queen or spade hits, I improve significantly. Checking keeps maximum flexibility going into the final hand while maintaining fold equity and showing value.

I was the preflop raiser and continuation-bettor on the flop; Claude called the flop and then checked the turn. My hand (T2o) has essentially no showdown value on J♣‑8♦‑6♥‑7♥ — only a 9 gives me a straight (4 outs) — so this is a pure position-based semi-bluff. Pot is 800 and Claude is relatively short (≈5.98k), so a strong turn bet can fold out single-pair hands and unpaired hands from his calling range while forcing him to pay poor odds to chase. Betting ~3/4 pot represents a made hand (Jx/overpairs) and preserves room to fold to big resistance. Plan: bet 600 to take the pot now; if he folds I win; if he calls I re-evaluate on the river and will fold to a sizable check-raise (showing sets/Jx), but can barrel a safe river if it’s a blank or call down thin if I pick up additional equity.

Short-stacked on hand 149 of 150 with Q♠4♠ on 8♦6♥J♣7♥. Facing 600 chip bet with 5,980 stack. Flush draw provides 11 outs (~23% equity) against effective pot odds requiring 30%, making this marginally unprofitable on pure EV. However, tournament context is critical: with only 1 hand remaining and 5,380 chips after folding, I'd be dangerously underfunded for final hand. Taking calculated risks with legitimate draws is essential for short-stack survival in endgame tournament play. Calling 600 to win potentially 2,000 (1,400 current pot + 600 call) gives me realistic equity to improve or win immediately. This balances chip accumulation needs with hand equity, positioning me better for the final hand regardless of outcome.

River decision with Q♠4♠ on 8♦6♥J♣7♥3♦. I hold queen-high with no pair made—a marginal hand at best on this board. With only one hand remaining after this, chip preservation is critical. 

Key analysis:
- My hand: Queen-high (weakest possible completing hand)
- Board: Dry board with no flush draws completing, no straight possibilities
- Opponent likely has a pair or similar marginal strength given the betting pattern
- Stack consideration: 5,380 chips with one final hand to play

Given that action appears to be to me after the river (To Call: 0), the optimal play is to CHECK. This accomplishes:
1. Reaches showdown with my queen-high
2. Preserves all remaining chips for the final hand
3. Avoids over-committing with a marginal hand in a heads-up situation
4. Maximizes my chip count going into Hand #150 regardless of this hand's outcome

Betting or raising with Q-high is not viable given the weak nature of the hand. Showdown allows me to see the result and advance to the final hand with whatever chips remain.

I was the preflop aggressor and barreled flop and turn; my hand (T♦2♣) has essentially no showdown value on J♣–8♦–6♥–7♥–3♦. Claude called both my flop and a large turn bet, which strongly polarizes his range toward at least a single pair (Jx, 8x, 6x, 7x), two pair/sets, or even a completed straight (T9) — hands that will almost always call a river bluff. The pot is 2,000 and Claude is relatively short, so a river bet (even large) would get called by most of his calling range and fold equity is very thin. I have a huge stack advantage and nothing to gain by risking chips on a low-EV bluff in this spot. Best play is to conserve chips and check back; if he bets into me I can fold and preserve my tournament equity. Action: check.

Pocket tens in heads-up on the button is a strong raising hand. With a chip deficit (7,330 vs 32,520), I need to play aggressively with premium hands to build my stack. Raising to 300 applies pressure and takes down the blinds a reasonable portion of the time while building the pot when called. This is optimal play for the final hand.

Heads-up in the big blind with QQ — one of the best hands preflop. Claude opened to 300 from the button; pot is 400 and I need 200 to call. I want to take the initiative, isolate the button and extract value from worse hands (AK, AQ, smaller pairs) while denying cheap odds to speculative holdings. I will 3-bet to a size that applies pressure but doesn’t commit my entire stack: raise to 1500 total (i.e. put 1500 in the pot). That sizing should fold some weaker broadways, get called by worse, and still leave both stacks with room for postflop play (if he calls he’ll have roughly ~5.6k behind). Postflop plan: continue as the aggressor — c-bet favorable textures and evaluate further action if he 4-bets or jams.

Final hand of tournament. Short-stacked 7,080 vs 31,120. Pocket tens is a strong hand in heads-up poker. After Minni's 3-bet to 1,500, I'm committed to going all-in. With limited chips, I need to accumulate with premium hands. TT is ahead of many of Minni's potential 3-bet hands (broadway cards, small pairs, etc.). Pushing all-in for 7,080 total is the correct aggressive short-stack play with a strong hand in the final hand.

Claude responded to my 3-bet with a large 4-bet to 7,080. Heads-up QQ is a premium — too strong to fold to a single 4-bet but not strong enough to 5-bet jam profitably against a polarized 4-bet (villain can have AA/KK and some bluffs). Calling 5,580 more (7080 total) costs ~18% of my stack to contest a ~8,580 pot now (pot after my call ≈ 14,160), so I only need ~39% equity to break even — reasonable against a mixed 4-bet range. I’ll call to see a flop, keep worse hands and bluffs in the pot, and play postflop depending on texture (c-bet/continue on favorable boards, pot-control or fold to heavy action on ace/king-heavy runouts).

I called Claude’s huge 4‑bet preflop, but this flop is exactly the texture that punishes calling off with QQ: K♦ 2♥ 7♥ hits Claude’s 4‑betting range hard (AA/KK/AK are heavily represented) and only leaves me ahead of rare bluffs and some small pairs. With a ~14,160 pot already and a king on board, I want to keep the pot under control and avoid bloating the pot out of position with a hand that is usually behind. Checking preserves showdown control, lets me induce bluffs or get a cheaper look at the turn, and keeps weaker hands and bluffs in that I can extract value from later. Plan: check here; call small‑to‑medium continuation bets (~≤1/3 pot ≈ 4.7k) to see the turn and re‑evaluate, fold to large sizing/overbets that strongly represent K/AA/KK, and if Claude checks back I’ll consider a small bet on a blank turn to start extracting from Ax/AJ type hands while still controlling pot size.

Already all-in from pre-flop aggression with pocket tens. Minni checked on the flop with Kd 2h 7h board. With no chips remaining to act, I can only check and let the hand play out to completion on the turn and river. This is the final hand of the tournament.

Pot = 14,160. I have QQ on K♦ 2♥ 7♥ 3♥. Claude 4‑bet preflop, so his range is heavily weighted to AA/KK/AK (and very few bluffs). He checked the flop, so he’s either pot‑controlling with a strong hand or slow‑playing — I shouldn’t bloat the pot out of position with a likely‑behind hand, but I can still try a controlled probe. The 3♥ on the turn is a ‘‘blank’’ relative to A/K (it does make 3 hearts on board but a made flush requires two hearts in his hand — very few combos), so I’ll fire a small value/probe bet to charge worse Ax/medium pairs and deny a free river. I want to keep the sizing manageable and retain an easy fold option if Claude check‑raises. Bet ~1/3 pot to extract value while controlling risk; fold to a large check‑raise, call small calls and re‑evaluate the river.

Final hand of tournament. With pocket tens and facing a 300 chip call into an 18,860 chip pot (63:1 odds), I must call. Folding surrenders immediately. The pot odds are exceptional and TT has reasonable equity against Minni's turn betting range. No fold option exists when the tournament ends this hand.
